Chicago · Pesach · Weather History

Fifty Chicago Passovers, with weather, date-linked history, and a matzah price estimate.

This table covers the last 50 completed Passovers in Chicago, from 1976 through 2025. Each row includes the first two festival days in the diaspora calendar, observed Chicago weather, two linked "on this day" events, and an estimated nominal price for a regular 1 lb box of Manischewitz Original Passover Matzo.

Coverage
1976 through 2025
50 completed Chicago Passovers
Warmest Day 1
1976: 84 F
Thu, Apr 15, 1976
Snowiest Reading
1982
1.3" snow
Estimated Box Price
$0.70 to $3.94
inflation-scaled estimate

Method

Passover dates come from Hebcal's diaspora holiday API. Weather is from Chicago daily-history pages at Extreme Weather Watch, with a NOAA daily-summaries fallback for the missing 1980 April 1-2 rows. News coverage uses up to ten same-date New York Times articles per festival day, ranked from the official NYT Archive API with an Article Search fallback only when needed; summaries are trimmed to about 100 words. Matzah prices are estimated by scaling a current shelf price with annual CPI values published from BLS data at OfficialData.

Matzah Estimate

  • Anchor price: $3.99 from Good Eggs for Manischewitz Original Passover Matzo, 1 lb.
  • Inflation series: annual U.S. CPI values compiled from BLS data at OfficialData.
  • Each year is scaled against the current 2026 CPI value shown in that table.

Kerik Contact Is Said to Lead to Demotion of Jail Official
Thomas Antenen, long-time spokesman in New York City government, is asked to step down as Corrections Department Deputy Commissioner after evidence shows he discussed city inquiry with former Police Commissioner Bernard B Kerik after being ordered not discuss matter; prosecutors, investigating Kerik regarding real estate dealings in city, happened to record conversation between two men (M)

As Budget Pain Filters Down, New Yorkers Start to Cry Out
Impact of Gov George E Pataki's vetos and budget cuts begin to be felt across New York Sstate as students scramble to get tuition paid and health care industry tries to figure out how to take care of services for those cut from assistance programs; Senate majority leader Joseph L Bruno speaks out against cuts that Pataki holds are essential to maintaining financial stability in state; photo (M)

More Retired Generals Call for Rumsfeld's Resignation
The widening circle of retired generals who have stepped forward to call for Defense Secretary Donald H. Rumsfeld's resignation is shaping up as an unusual outcry that could pose a significant challenge to Mr. Rumsfeld's leadership, current and former generals said on Thursday. Maj. Gen. Charles H. Swannack Jr., who led troops on the ground in Iraq as recently as 2004 as the commander of the Army's 82nd Airborne Division, on Thursday became the fifth retired senior general in recent days to call publicly for Mr. Rumsfeld's ouster. Also Thursday, another retired Army general, Maj. Gen. John Riggs, joined in...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
3 Deaths in China Reveal Disparity in Price of Lives
He Qingzhi's teenage daughter, Yuan, and her two friends lived on the same street near the Yangtze River, attended the same middle school and were crushed to death in the same traffic accident late last year. After that, the symmetry ended: under Chinese law, Yuan's life was worth less than the others'. Mr. He, 38, who has lived in this town in central China for 15 years, was told that his neighbors were entitled to roughly three times more compensation from the accident because they were registered urban residents while he was only a migrant worker.

POPE BENEDICT XVI: Roots of Conservatism; Turbulence on Campus in 60's Hardened Views of Future Pope
For all Pope Benedict XVI's decades as a Vatican insider, it may have been the crucible of a university town swept by student radicalism in the late 1960's that definitively shaped the man who now leads the Roman Catholic Church. During his Bavarian childhood under the Nazis, Joseph Ratzinger became convinced that the moral authority based in Catholic teachings was the sole reliable bulwark against human barbarism, according to friends, associates, and his biographer, John L. Allen Jr.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Acela, Built to Be Rail's Savior, Bedevils Amtrak at Every Turn
It was called the American Flyer, and its goals were ambitious: to speed train travel between Northeastern cities, steal customers from air shuttles, provide the model for a nationwide fast rail system and help its deficit-prone parent, Amtrak, earn a profit. ''These trains will enable Amtrak to carry its customers into the 21st century aboard 21st-century trains,'' said Thomas M. Downs, Amtrak's president, at a 1996 ceremony announcing a $611 million contract for the new trains.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Evidence in Vioxx Suits Shows Intervention by Merck Officials
In 2000, amid rising concerns that its painkiller Vioxx posed heart risks, Merck overruled one of its own scientists after he suggested that a patient in a clinical trial had probably died of a heart attack. In an e-mail exchange about Vioxx, the company's most important new drug at the time, a senior Merck scientist repeatedly urged the researcher to change his views about the death ''so that we don't raise concerns.'' In later reports to the Food and Drug Administration and in a paper published in 2003, Merck listed the cause of death as ''unknown'' for the patient, a...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Medicare Change Will Limit Access to Claim Hearing
A new federal policy will make it significantly more difficult for Medicare beneficiaries to obtain hearings in person before a judge when the government denies their claims for home care, nursing home services, prescription drugs and other treatments. For years, hearings have been held at more than 140 Social Security offices around the country. In July, the Department of Health and Human Services will take over the responsibility, and department officials said all judges would then be located at just four sites -- in Cleveland; Miami; Irvine, Calif.; and Arlington, Va.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Sousa? Many Students March to Mariachi Instead
At home with his family four brothers and a foster mother -- Jorge Geraldo struggles with pimples and shyness, a handsome 18-year-old with deep brown eyes who sleeps on Goofy and Donald Duck sheets that tend to lie in an unmade heap on his bunk bed. But come the weekend, he dons his traje de charro -- the suit of the horseman, a glimmering costume with gold buttons slithering up the sides and custom-fitted by a tailor in nearby Tijuana -- to become the lead singer in Mariachi Chula Vista, a group of high school mariachi musicians who have forsaken...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Many Say End of Firearm Ban Changed Little
Despite dire predictions that the streets would be awash in military-style guns, the expiration of the decade-long assault weapons ban last September has not set off a sustained surge in the weapons' sales, gun makers and sellers say. It also has not caused any noticeable increase in gun crime in the past seven months, according to several metropolitan police departments. The uneventful expiration of the assault weapons ban did not surprise gun owners, nor did it surprise some advocates of gun control. Rather, it underscored what many of them had said all along: that the ban was porous -- so...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
A Son's Grief, in Word and Watercolor
Max Miller has been saying Kaddish for his father, Murray, and, being an artist, he is observing ritual his own way, traveling from synagogue to synagogue throughout New York and beyond, reciting daily prayer and then painting watercolor study of synagogue in which he has recited it; has visited some 50 synagogues over last 10 months; drawing (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Forged by Fire
New York City Health and Mental Hygiene Dept celebrates its 200th anniversary; was born to battle outbreaks of yellow fever in 1805, and it has been the big threats, from smallpox to bioterrorism, that have fueled agency's growth, transforming it into biggest, most active and richest municipal health department; Dr Thomas R Frieden, current commissioner, comments; photos; graph; drawing (L)

QUOTATION OF THE DAY
''Back in the early 90's, criminals wanted those Rambo-type weapons they could brandish. Today they are much happier with a 9-millimeter handgun they can stick in their belt.'' JIM PASCO, executive director of the Fraternal Order of Police. [1]
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
For Indonesia's Tsunami Amputees, a Long, Hard Road
As he knelt to pray at his son's grave, Zaini's left trouser cuff trailed in the red dirt, a hollow sheath of fabric where his leg once was. His aluminum crutches lay nearby. A survivor of the Dec. 26 tsunami that took his entire family -- his wife and 10 children -- and smashed his home and fishing boat, Mr. Zaini, 60, also lost his infected left leg in an amputation that Australian doctors said was necessary to save his life.

Rice and Cheney Are Said to Push Iraqi Politicians on Stalemate
Bush administration abandons its hands-off approach to Iraqi politics; presses top Kurdish and Shiite politicians to end their stalemate over forming new government with personal appeals from Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice and Vice Pres Dick Cheney; there is concern about spike in mayhem from emboldened insurgency as insurgents unleash devastating technique: twin double bombings at police academy in Tikrit and ice cream parlor in Shiite neighborhood of Baghdad that kills 21 and wounds scores more; in both attacks, second bomb detonates within minutes after first, killing and wounding policemen and bystanders rushing to care for victims of initial blasts...

Transit News Is a Click Away, and About 5 Years Behind Schedule
The good news for commuters is that a new, publicly financed Web site established this month offers up-to-date information on highway, traffic and transit conditions in the New York City region. But the road to the site's creation has been long and circuitous.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
In City's Push for Stadium, Silver's District Reaps Benefits
Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg has a golf date planned with Assembly Speaker Sheldon Silver on Thursday. More than eagles and birdies, the between-holes talk among the two will almost certainly be about whether Mr. Silver will sign off on the mayor's plan for a West Side stadium. It is no coincidence that the stadium's future happens to depend in large part upon Mr. Silver, who effectively controls a third of the state's Public Authorities Control Board, which has final say over the stadium plan and could vote on its future as early as May 18.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
A Tax Benefit for Big Donors Often Bypasses Idea of Charity
Regulators and lawmakers push for tighter rules on so-called supporting organizations as part of broader crackdown on charitable tax exmptions; supporting organizations, created to support specific charity or charities, are attractive to donors because they offer generous tax benefits associated with donating directly to charities and operate much like private foundations, but without foundation's more onerous requirements; donors get perks because they agree to relinquish control over money; since they appoint organization's board, they can retain great deal of influence over it; there is suspicion that many wealthy people use these organizations more for tax planning than for any charitable...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
A Hundred Cellphones Bloom, and Chinese Take to the Streets
Anti-Japanese protests of thousands of people in China, fueled by e-mail, text messaging, instant online messaging and cellphones, seen as possible harbinger of future popular movements; China is now largest cellphone market, with nearly 350 million users; number of Internet users is about 100 million and growing at 30 percent a year; result is constant tension between population hungry for freer communication and government that regards information control as essential to its power; few countries censor information and communications as tightly as China; government is said to have powerful filtering devices that can screen cellphone and e-mail messages; growing number...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Dreams, Hunches and 600-to-1 Keep the Numbers Game Going
Illegal numbers games quietly persist 25 years after New York State lottery was created; underground numbers games have endured because street pays better odds than state, tax-free, and mony speaks every language in city; its attraction is promise of tiny windfall, enough to change life a bit for a short while; one organization that controlled 80 numbers spots around New York City generated $5 million a year in profits before being dismantled; basic structure of games has been same for generations; player picks three-digit number and bets anywhere from fistful of coins to $30 that it will be chosen as...

Bloodied Marines Sound Off About Want of Armor and Men
Marine Corps Company E, which suffered highest casualty rate of any company in war in Iraq, returns home complaining about lack of armor and shortage of men and planning, special report, The Company: Six Months in Ramadi; leaders and infantrymen break institutional code of silence to tell of their destroyed morale and of ruined careers of some of their fiercest warriors; more than one-third of unit's 185 troops were killed or wounded, having endured 26 firefights, 90 mortar attacks and more than 90 homemade bombs; unit had less than half the troops now doing its job in Ramadi, and resorted...

Credit Receipts for DeLay Trip Raise Concerns
House Republicans say they will redouble their efforts to resolve partisan impasse over ethics committee even as Rep Tom DeLay faces new disclosures about his overseas travel that could eventually come before panel; Speaker J Dennis Hastert said to be driven more by institutional concerns than by furor engulfing DeLay; Democrats say Republican majority should undo rules at center of feud that have paralyzed ethics committee; under one new rule, ethics complaints could be automatically dismissed if evenly divided ethics committee does not agree on how to proceed after 45 days; DeLay was admonished by ethics panel in three instances...

For New York's Bagel Shops, Passover Week Is No Festival
Terrace Bagels in Brooklyn is bracing for the annual spring slowdown, its worst week all year. Sidewalk vendors on the Upper West Side say they'll cut their bagel orders by as much as 50 percent.

9/11 Panel Plans Hard Questions About the F.B.I. and Justice Dept.
Atty Gen John Ashcroft and former FBI director Louis J Freeh, who will testify before 9/11 commission next week, are expected to be criticized for their agencies' failures before terror attacks; commission members say hearings will bolster what will be major recommendation of panel's final report: an overhaul of domestic counterintelligence agency separate from FBI; Bush administration opposes such a move; Ashcroft and Freeh have defended their efforts before 9/11 in testimony to other investigators and in other forums; FBI insists it has made broad changes in its counterterrorism division since attacks, but Thomas H Kean, chairman of commission, voices...

GENERALS IN IRAQ CONSIDER OPTIONS FOR MORE TROOPS
American commanders may send more forces to Iraq if situation worsens, and Bush administration officials say new surge of violence by Shiites represents challenge to plans to turn over power in less than 90 days; Pres Bush says he intends to stick to June 30 date, even as he concedes that structure for new government has not been settled; vows that violence instigated by Shiite cleric Moktada al-Sadr would be put down; says he is committed to 'staying the course' despite new wave of attacks on American forces, in which Shiites as well as Sunnis have now joined; senior military...

Today's Lesson for College Students: Lighten Up
Colleges across country are paying ever-increasing attention to undergraduates' personal growth and emotional well-being; they are responding to rising number of students seeking help for stress-related conditions on campus and expectations of consumer-minded parents; many colleges are extending therapeutic culture far beyond treatment for clinical depression and bipolar disorders; they are offering wide range of services and activities intended to help students negotiate what used to be considered ordinary rites of passage, including homesickness, sophomore angst and romantic relationships; colleges are offering free massages, dogs to cuddle in exam season, biofeedback workshops and therapists available to help students work through...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Security Rules Tie Foreigners With Red Tape
Thousands of skilled foreign professionals working legally in United States are confonting ballooning immigration-services backlog, with mostly routine paperwork they need to work and travel taking many months to obtain; processing times for everything from renewing annual work permit to securing permanent legal residence have as much as quadrupled over last 18 months, despite Bush administration's pledge to cut waiting times in half; wait to replace lost green card has grown to 19 months from four; document allowing foreign worker to re-enter country after brief trip takes seven months instead of two; as result, number of pending applications has risen...

Now Shaping Minds, Instead of Buildings
Former New York State Urban Development Corporation head Richard Kahan has co-founded Urban Assembly, nonprofit organization that helps to set up small theme-based public schools; uses his experience in city planning to redesign New York City high schools and other buildings to better suit smaller, specialized schools like High School for Law Government and Justice; photos (M)

U.S. SEEKS ARREST OF SHIITE CLERIC
American officials in Iraq confirm they have issued arrest warrant for Moktada al-Sadr, militant Shiite cleric whose followers started uprising in six Iraqi cities over weekend; Sadr says he would 'welcome' showdown with American forces; has pledged to drive them out of Iraq; warrant for his arrest is issued in connection with shooting and hacking to death of rival Shiite cleric at mosque in Najaf last Apr; announcement, months after warrant was secretly approved, seems timed to coincide with new American military offensive; American troops appear to be moving into area around Kufa, where Sadr's followers have seized control and...

Los Angeles Times Wins 5 Pulitzer Prizes and Wall Street Journal Wins 2
Los Angeles Times wins five Pulitzer Prizes to dominate news competition; wins Pulitzer for breaking news reporting and national reporting; photographer Carolyn Cole wins for feature photography; columnist Bill Stall wins for editorial writing; Daniel Neil is first automobile critic to win Pulitzer for criticism; Wall Street Journal wins two prizes; reporters Kevin Helliker and Thomas M Burton win for explanatory reporting and Daniel Golden wins for beat reporting; Dallas Morning News photographers David Leeson and Cheryl Diaz Meyer win for breaking news photography; Washington Post reporter Anthony Shadid wins for international reporting; New York Times wins for public service...

Up to 12 Marines Die in Raid on Their Base As Fierce Fighting Spreads to 6 Iraqi Cities
American forces in Iraq come under fierce attack; up to 12 marines are killed in Ramadi, while Shiite militiamen loyal to rebel cleric Moktada al-Sadr step up three-day-old assault in Najaf; American warplanes fire rockets and houses in Falluja, where American security contractors were killed and their bodies mutilated last week; marines drive armored columns into heart of city, where they fight block by block to flush out insurgents, making several arrests; violence hits some six cities, with heaviest fighting raging in Sunni strongholds of Falluja and Ramadi; role of most of majority Shiites previously thought to be relatively sympathetic...

A Bit of Arthritis Aside, an Old Fireboat is Fitted for the Future
Navigation system of 65-year-old New York City fireboat is upgraded with computer and sophisticated programs intended to cut 10 minutes off average response time; photos (M)

About New York; Playing a New York Oddball (Wait, Is He Playing?)
Craig Schwartz, member of Screen Actors Guild, has appeared in about 30 movies filmed in New York City, usually playing scruffy, homeless type with off-beat personality, much like himself; is known to casts and crews as radioman because he always has boom-box radio on strap across his chest; photo (M)

Studies in Chicago Fault Policy of Holding Back 3rd Graders
University of Chicago studies find that nine-year effort to end social promotion in Chicago public schools has been expensive and yielded few benefits; say plan does not help third graders, increases special education placements and leads to higher dropout rate for students forced to repeat eighth grade; officials reply that schools have improved over all; officials in New York City, who are using Chicago plan as model for their own efforts to revamp city's failing school system, say they are adjusting to eliminate flaws in Chicago plan (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Trading in Lies, Some Informers Build Cases For Prosecutors
Prosecutors acknowledge dangers of using cooperating witnesses, who may be seeking leniency for their own crimes, after Judge John Gleeson overturns gang-murder conviction of Angel M DeAngelo in Brooklyn after determining that three men who testified against him perjured themselves; say that cooperators are needed to get to people on inside, especially in organized crime and gang cases; are convinced that DeAngelo is killer (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
At Word of U.S. Foray, a Baghdad Militia Erupts
Shiite neighborhood in central Baghdad quickly mobilizes for war in response to report that American soldiers raided office of radical cleric Moktada al-Sadr and tore up poster of his father, revered martyr; incident highlights spreading power vacuum in Iraq, with no American soldiers or Iraqi police present during disturbance; shows depth of support for Sadr, with thousands of men and boys ready to fight for him in just one neighborhood; photos (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Since '94 Horror, Rwandans Turn Toward Islam
Many Rwandans, disgusted by role some priests and nuns played in 1994 massacres of 800,000 people, are shunning organized religion, while many more have turned to Islam; Islam is fastest growing religion in still predominantly Catholic Rwanda; Muslim community now boasts so many converts that it is on crash campaign to build new mosques; Muslim leaders credit doubling of their numbers, to about 15 percent of population, to their ability to shield most Muslims, and many other Rwandans, during massacres; photos (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
City Decides Abuse Victim Is Not to Blame
New York City will will withdraw allegations that injuries and damages sustained by victim of sex abuse at Boys Choir of Harlem were caused in whole or in part by boy's culpable conduct; Frank Jones Jr, counselor at academy, was convicted of molesting boy; family is suing city for $5 million (M)

Muslims Hesitating on Gifts As U.S. Scrutinizes Charities
Many American Muslims are eager to contribute to relief effort in Iraq but are afraid to donate to Islamic charities, or to donate as much as they would like, because of scrutiny by FBI; four American Muslim charities have been closed over accusations of ties to terrorist groups and several prominent Muslim donors have been indicted or detained; leaders of about 20 Islamic charities will meet with Treasury Dept representative to try to figure out how to clarify to donors which charities are legitimate and complying with government regulations; photo (M)

2 M.T.A. Officials Fired in December in Bribery Inquiry
MTA quietly fired two veteran executives in December on evidence that they took bribes to allow contractors to submit fake bills; prosecutors weigh charges; I-Lite Electric says more than $100,000 was paid to Howard Weissman, who was dismissed along with deputy, Ronald Allan; inflated construction costs have emerged as significant financial drain on agency that is raising fares to close yawning budget gap; cost of renovating Manhattan headquarters has risen from $135 million to more than $430 million over past few years, in part because of widespread fraud that has led to eight arrests; Weissman oversaw two dozen buildings and...

BUSH URGING U.N. TO LIFT SANCTIONS IMPOSED ON IRAQ
Pres Bush calls on United Nations to lift economic sanctions on Iraq now that Saddam Hussein's regime has 'passed into history'; ending or suspending sanctions should make it easier to sell Iraqi oil and raise money to repair physical damage from war and economic damage from years of international isolation as well as neglect by Hussein; Security Council must vote on such issues; this could set stage for more debate, if not another diplomatic battle with France, Germany and Russia over administration of postwar Iraq, status of commercial contracts with Iraq and awarding of reconstruction contracts; Bush administration officials have...

U.S. Bombs Iranian Guerrilla Forces Based in Iraq
American forces bomb bases of Mujahedeen Khalq, armed Iranian opposition group that has maintained thousands of fighters along Iraq's border with Iran for more than decade; Bush adminstration says group, which has been labeled terrorist organization by US since 1997, supported Saddam Hussein's military; main beneficiary of strikes will be Iranian government, which has lost many soldiers in recent years to cross-border attacks by guerrillas seeking to overthrow Iran's Islamic government; more than 150 members of Congress support group, describing it as pro-democracy, antifundamentalist, antiterrorist and helpful to US in providing information about activities of Iranian regime; senior administration officials...

Baghdad Bank Plundered as Iraq's Economy Falters
Armed gangs are plundering Iraq's central bank in Baghdad, cracking open safes and hauling out cash; with banking system devastated, value of dinar has plunged; country's economy, almost completely dependent upon government, has come to virtual standstill and all transactions are conducted in cash, further limiting amount of business that can be done; oil fields that accounted for most of country's economic output are not operating and upgrading them will be expensive; oil revenues will not likely be enough to meet all the competing demands for cash; Iraq has foreign debts of more than $200 billion, amount that will drain...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U.S. Generals Meet in Palace, Sealing Victory
Gen Tommy R Franks seals his victory in war on Iraq by convening top allied officers in one of Saddam Hussein's proudest palaces; commanders gathering at Abu Ghraib palace in Baghdad discuss how to maintain security and rebuild Iraq now that Hussein is gone; decide to meet again in Franks's headquarters in Qatar in several days to complete plans; agree that Army will take responsibility for Baghdad and territory to north, while Marines deploy in south, where British control Basra, most important city; two-hour session concludes with video conference with Pres Bush; meeting is laden with symbolism, making clear that...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Day 2 · Fri, Apr 18, 2003
Titles10The Renaissance of White Plains; A Wallflower of a City Becomes the Belle of the BallRebuilding Contract, Threats to Syria From Congress, Help for a MuseumTrained for War, 12 Green Berets Keep the Peace in an Iraqi TownDocuments Show Urgent Iraqi Push to Recruit and Control TroopsOfficer Killed, Another Is Hurt In a Shooting In New JerseyU.S. GIVES BECHTEL A MAJOR CONTRACT IN REBUILDING IRAQOnce Just Threats, Cutbacks Sink In Among New YorkersRunaway Bus Ravages 3 Queens Blocks, Injuring 9Fearing SARS, Ontario Urges Wider QuarantinesFerry Operator Is the Target Of U.S. Inquiry
The Renaissance of White Plains; A Wallflower of a City Becomes the Belle of the Ball
White Plains, NY, is undergoing economic renaissance under leadership of Mayor Joseph Delfino; has become boomtown, with billion dollars' worth of construction under way, including more than 1,600 rental apartments in and around central business district; 15 movie screens, new restaurants and major retailers from Target to Fortunoff are in works; developers keep proposing more: hotel, condominiums, even office space; photos; map (L)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Rebuilding Contract, Threats to Syria From Congress, Help for a Museum
Summary of major events in Iraq on April 17 (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Trained for War, 12 Green Berets Keep the Peace in an Iraqi Town
US Army Special Forces soldiers in Diwaniya, who came to Iraq trained to fight war, find themselves wading into angry crowds, mediating between rival tribes and trying to hold together thin threads of social order; photo; 12-man team has become de facto center of government, hearing daily from tribal leaders, businesses and ordinary citizens who stream into compound to complain about spotty electricity, rampant looting and lack of jobs and commerce; Green Berets, several of whom speak Arabic, have in last week started police force, recruited English-speaking professor as city manager, located offices for municipal government and begun holding meetings...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Documents Show Urgent Iraqi Push to Recruit and Control Troops
Documents found in Baath Party bureau in Zubayr show effort during summer of 2002 to mobilize cadres across country; efforts included emptying prisons in attempt to spark loyalty and recruit troops; party collected declarations from Iraqis professing willingness to make suicide attacks; documents represent important record of Baath Party culture and tactics it used to galvanize and control its security forces; what is impossible to convey in documents, however, is fear that existed between party leaders and soldiers whose lives, and lives of their families, depended on absolute obedience; documents illustrate patterns of coercion and redundancy showing conformity and discipline...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Officer Killed, Another Is Hurt In a Shooting In New Jersey
Police officer from Fair Lawn, NJ, is killed and Clifton police officer is shot in Fair Lawn; dead officer is a woman; unknown number of suspects flee in Fair Lawn officer's marked police car, which is recovered in Paterson (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U.S. GIVES BECHTEL A MAJOR CONTRACT IN REBUILDING IRAQ
Bush administration gives Bechtel Group first major contract in vast reconstruction plan for Iraq that assigns no major role to UN or Europe; US AID contract, potentially worth $680 million over 18 months, sets off heated contest among politically connected US companies and is part of multibillion-dollar effort to stabilize country and set up new government; US taxpayers to pay initial costs, but Iraqi oil revenue is supposed to eventually pay for much of reconstruction; retired US Gen Jay Garner to run civil administration until interim Iraqi authority is in place to work with World Bank and IMF; European countries...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Once Just Threats, Cutbacks Sink In Among New Yorkers
New Yorkers begin to confront long-threatened budget cutbacks, visible in dirtier streets, more homeless people in subways and job losses, after months of dire predictions by Mayor Michael Bloomberg; several comment; photos (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Runaway Bus Ravages 3 Queens Blocks, Injuring 9
Speeding Green Lines bus zigzags down three blocks of residential street in Queens, smashing into 15 occupied and parked vehicles, knocking down utility poles and sending terrified pedestrians flying; nine people are injured, none seriously; had only driver and one passenger aboard; police say driver might have suffered seizure; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Fearing SARS, Ontario Urges Wider Quarantines
Ontario health officials take extraordinary step of asking anyone with even one symptom of SARS to stay home for few days to avoid possibly spreading severe respiratory ailment during Easter holiday weekend; Public Safety Commissioner Dr James Young and Health Min Tony Clement explain; Canada, particularly Toronto, is hardest-hit country outside Asia: 12 of 126 cases have been fatal, death rate of 9.5 percent; Toronto area cases traced to Scarborough Grace Hospital; outbreak centers on Philippines-based Bukas-Loob Sa Diyos Covenant Community, Roman Catholic group; Pennsylvania may now have SARS case in patient who attended religious meeting in Toronto on March...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Ferry Operator Is the Target Of U.S. Inquiry
Federal authorities are investigating allegations that region's leading private ferry company, New York Waterway, defrauded federal government of hundreds of thousands of dollars in aftermath of Sept 11 attack; are looking into whether ferry company submitted inflated bills to Port Authority of New York and New Jersey for ferry services it provided to replace commuter rail link between New Jersey and Lower Manhattan that was destroyed by terror attack; New York Waterway receives about $1.74 million a month in subsidies (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search

Day 1 · Thu, Mar 28, 2002
Titles10Taking the Plunge, Gown and All; 'Incubator' Adds Business Know-How to Fashion SenseNot the Best of Times, but Manhattan Hotels See Encouraging SignsAt Columbia, Police Recruiters Draw Little Interest, Good or BadBOMB KILLS AT LEAST 19 IN ISRAEL AS ARABS MEET OVER PEACE PLANPresident Signs Bill On Campaign Gifts; Begins Money TourRacial Gap in Test Scores Found Across New YorkFor Priests, Days of Hope in Time of TroubleSaudi in Strong Plea to Israel and ArabsBudget Plan By McGreevey Raises DoubtsCouncil Leader Visiting Places That Budget Cuts Would Hurt
Taking the Plunge, Gown and All; 'Incubator' Adds Business Know-How to Fashion Sense
'Incubator' boutiques, featuring fashions of young unknown designers, are established by business improvement groups on Lower East Side and in Harlem to promote shopping areas and help offset start-up costs that cause failure of many fledgling stores; photos of Forward on Orchard Street and The Brownstone on Fifth Avenue near 125th Street (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Not the Best of Times, but Manhattan Hotels See Encouraging Signs
Manhattan hotels see rebound after dark days that followed Sept 11 terrorist attack; average occupancy rate in Feb was 74 percent, only one percentage point lower than in Feb 2001; hotels that belong to chains, like W Hotel in Times Square, are faring better because larger companies have more ways to cut costs; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
At Columbia, Police Recruiters Draw Little Interest, Good or Bad
New York Police Department representatives draw poor turnout at Columbia University on first day of Ivy League recruitment campaign; photo (S)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
BOMB KILLS AT LEAST 19 IN ISRAEL AS ARABS MEET OVER PEACE PLAN
Palestinian suicide bomber blows himself up in crowded hotel dining roon in Netanya, Israel, just as more than 200 people gather for Passover holiday meal, killing at least 19 and wounding more than 100 others, many of them children; militant Islamic group Hamas takes responsibility; Israel vows swift response to what Israeli government spokesman calls 'Passover massacre'; officials suggest that mission of Gen Anthony C Zinni, American mediator, has been severely undermined; State Dept says Zinni will remain in area and continue working' Pres Bush scores 'cold-blooded killing' and calls for end to such 'callous' acts; Palestinian Authority condemns attack...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
President Signs Bill On Campaign Gifts; Begins Money Tour
Pres Bush signs campaign finance reform bill into law without ceremony, then leaves Washington for two-day, three-state trip to raise nearly $4 million for Republican candidates and his party; says he is not 'really unhappy' with bill, but issues statement critical of its major provisions; quiet, no-cameras signing, with fund-raising tour as follow-up, underscores how little enthusiasm he has for bill that had been championed by Sen John McCain, his rival for presidential nomination in 2000; some Republicans are fuming that Bush stood aloof from their efforts to kill politically popular measure in Congress; within hours of signing, Sen Mitch...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Racial Gap in Test Scores Found Across New York
New York State education officials find white and Asian elementary and middle school students do much better than black and Hispanic students in standardized English and math tests; this is first time state has broken down scores by race and ethnicity; findings also show that black and Hispanic students continue to lag as they go through school and that in many cases gap worsens; achievement gap by race and ethnicity exists across board, from affluent suburbs to large cities, but is most striking in urban areas with high concentrations of poverty, like New York City; statewide, there is gap of...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
For Priests, Days of Hope in Time of Trouble
Priests across nation observe Holy Week, most sacred season in Roman Catholic church, filled with mingling of emotions as never before as scandal over sexual abuse of children by fellow priests rages around them; scandal has brought them anger, shame, resentment and defiance; they say they feel tarred by crimes of few; they proclaim sympathy for victims, but feel for their offending colleagues; they feel undermined by their leaders; they are dismayed that many bishops appear to have turned blind eye to past abusers and some say bishops are now going overboard in punishing any priest with even whiff of...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Saudi in Strong Plea to Israel and Arabs
Crown Prince Abdullah of Saudi Arabia proposes to Arabs that they pledge as one to accept Israel as their neighbor if it meets three demands: withdrawal from all occupied territories, creation of Palestinian state, with capital in Jerusalem, and return of Palestinian refugees; prince's declaration seen as extraordinary appeal directed largely to Israeli people; it is attempt to rekindle desire for negotiated settlement by holding out possibility of 'normal relations' and security for Israel; prince's much promoted, much awaited speech is all but overshadowed by day of fresh violence in Israel and disarray of Arab meeting itself; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Budget Plan By McGreevey Raises Doubts
New Jersey legislators and Wall Street analysts warn that $23.66-billion spending plan proposed by Gov James McGreevey contains some of same flaws that have plagued state's budgets for years, such as understating actual spending, using overly high revenue predictions and one-time revenue sources; McGreevey, who promised to end state's bad fiscal habits, did acccomplish difficult balancing act by closing projected $5.3-billion shortfall without resorting to unpopular tax increases, but he is relying heavily on borrowing and nonrecurring revenues; his photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Council Leader Visiting Places That Budget Cuts Would Hurt
New York City Council Speaker Gifford Miller visits variety of sites likely to be hurt by budget squeeze; says tour is intended to assure residents that their concerns are understood; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Day 2 · Fri, Mar 29, 2002
Titles10Mideast Turmoil: The Fighting; Sharon Calls Arafat an Enemy and Sends Tanks To Isolate Him at Headquarters In RamallahA National Challenged: The Bioterror Threat; Smallpox Vaccine Stockpile Is Larger Than Was ThoughtMideast Turmoil: Arab League; Arabs Approve an Offer to Israel With Conditions It Has RejectedA Nation Challenged: The Trade Center; Towers Fell as Intense Fire Beat Defenses, Report SaysMideast Turmoil: Baghdad; Baghdad-Kuwait Accord -- Support Is Rebuff to Bush's EffortsTaking Notes on Democracy; Tibetan Exiles Explore Government, the American WayA Political Neophyte in Newark Challenges a Shrewd IncumbentFirm That Advises Firefighter Widows Has Tarnished PastBrooklyn Councilman Is Charged in an Extortion SchemeThe Big City; The Perils Of Reform At Church
Mideast Turmoil: The Fighting; Sharon Calls Arafat an Enemy and Sends Tanks To Isolate Him at Headquarters In Ramallah
Israeli government declares Yasir Arafat an 'enemy' and sends tanks and armored personnel carriers to fully isolate him in his Ramallah headquarters and to launch 'extended operation in Palestinian territories; Prime Min Ariel Sharon, recalling deadly suicide bombing of Netanya hotel as well as two subsequent terrorist attacks, says no sovereign state can ignore such events; says 20,000 reserve soldiers are being called up to free regular forces for operation that could last long time; officials in Arafat's compound say tanks and armored personnel carriers have fully ringed them and bulldozers have breached hole in wall; gun battles break out...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
A National Challenged: The Bioterror Threat; Smallpox Vaccine Stockpile Is Larger Than Was Thought
Federal officials say they might have access to much more smallpox vaccine than was thought, finding that could change their options in responding to terrorist attack with smallpox; Aventis Pasteur, drug company, says it has more than 85 million doses of vaccine, which has been stored, frozen, for decades, and that tests show vaccine is still effective; most health officials do not advocate resumption of mass vaccination against smallpox, because vaccine itself can kill and cause injuries; but disclosure that vaccine supplies are more abundant than had been thought is likely to reignite debate about how government should prepare for...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Mideast Turmoil: Arab League; Arabs Approve an Offer to Israel With Conditions It Has Rejected
Arab nations commit themselves to accepting Israel as neighbor if it would meet set of conditions, many of which Israel has long rejected, leading to creation of independent Palestinian state; fact that proposal comes from Saudi Arabia and that even hard-line nations like Libya and Iraq joined consensus on issue illustrate distance region has moved since Arab countries punished Egypt for signing first Arab peace treaty with Israel by expelling it from Arab League and severing ties; this peace attempt, unlike previous Arab peace attempts, assures Israel 'normal relations' with Arabs if it takes certain steps toward ending conflict; it...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
A Nation Challenged: The Trade Center; Towers Fell as Intense Fire Beat Defenses, Report Says
Federal report on how twin towers fell on Sept 11 states that fireproofing, sprinkler systems and water supply for hoses were all disabled in face of blaze so intense that it drove temperatures as high as 2,000 degrees and generated heat equivalent to energy output of nuclear power plant; states that fire, combined with these failures, brought down towers even after they had shown surprising and lifesaving resiliency to massive structural damage caused by impact of two hijacked airliners; findings are contained in draft of report commissioned by Federal Emergency Management Agency and American Society of Civil Engineers; it describes...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Mideast Turmoil: Baghdad; Baghdad-Kuwait Accord -- Support Is Rebuff to Bush's Efforts
Arab League members declare that any attack on Iraq would be considered attack against all Arab states, giving Saddam Hussein broad Arab support in heading off any American military action against his country; in return, Iraq accepts policies it vehemently rejected in past, such as agreeing to recognize Kuwait as independent state and to not invade again; it also says it will work with United Nations in carrying out post-gulf war cease-fire provisions and would join Arab initiative, approved at Arab League meeting, that envisions peace with Israel; Crown Prince Abdullah of Saudi Arabia kisses Iraq's representative Izzat Ibrahim in...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Taking Notes on Democracy; Tibetan Exiles Explore Government, the American Way
Tibetan exiles Tenzin Norbu and Tsering Dorjee explore American system of government at behest of Dalai Lama, who is pushing his followers to establish strong political system for exile government based in Dharamsala, India; they are observing Connecticut General Assembly session and learning ins and outs of democracy (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
A Political Neophyte in Newark Challenges a Shrewd Incumbent
Newark (NJ) Mayor Sharpe James kicks off re-election campaign and enters race as favorite against Councilman Cory Booker; both candidates have raised enormous amounts of money for fiercely contested race; Booker, with Ivy League education, polished good looks and Wall Street backing, is political neophyte; issues and endorsements noted; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Firm That Advises Firefighter Widows Has Tarnished Past
Goldis Financial Group, firm that is advising many Sept 11 firefighters' widows, has record of questionable and, in some cases illegal conduct by some of its employees and business associates over past decade; regulators and federal prosecutors say two veteran Goldis brokers went to prison on federal charges for taking bribes to sell inflated stock to Goldis clients in 2000; FBI claims stock promoter who paid bribes is associated with Gambino crime family; financial records show that for years Goldis joined forces with some of industry's most notorious boiler rooms to sell high-risk securities known as penny stocks; broker from...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Brooklyn Councilman Is Charged in an Extortion Scheme
Brooklyn Councilman Angel Rodriguez is charged in federal indictment with demanding $1.5 million break on real estate deal from developer Gregory O'Connell in exchange for supporting multi-million dollar renovation of warehouse, which is to be converted to Fairway Supermarket; Rodriguez and friend Jonathan Morales signed contract with O'Connell to buy three Cobble Hill properties worth $2.5 million for $1 million, and, next day, Rodriguez voted in favor of supermarket development, which he had earlier opposed; also demanded $50,000 cash; both plead not guilty to extortion and related crimes; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
The Big City; The Perils Of Reform At Church
John Tierney The Big City column says Roman Catholic Church scandal is getting so much attention because church is large and newsworthy institution rather than because of its policies on celibacy; suggests that allowing women to become priests could eliminate some temptations, but bemoans depriving boys of special relationship with truly celibate men who have time to make difference in their lives (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search

Titles10Talk of Lost Farms Reflects Muddle of Estate Tax DebateNURSING SHORTAGE IS RAISING WORRIES ON PATIENTS' CARECHINESE INSISTING U.S. MUST DO MORE TO END STANDOFFDarwin vs. Design: Evolutionists' New BattleSins of the Father, Visited Upon the MotherCHINA BUILDUP PUTS TAIWAN ON EDGERead All About ItQUOTATION OF THE DAYIn a Colorful Campaign in Peru, 3 Points of View EmergeBrontë Rapture, Far From the Moors
Talk of Lost Farms Reflects Muddle of Estate Tax Debate
Article on reality of estate taxes, which is far different from mythology in which family farmers have become icons for abolition campaign; IRS analysis for 1999 finds that almost no actual working farmers owe anything in estate taxes, Iowa State economist Neal Harl has never found case in which farm was lost for this reason, and even American Farm Bureau Federation, major advocate of repeal, cannot cite single example of farm lost for estate taxes; example is Harlyn Riekena, who owns 950 acres of Iowa farmland but expects to owe nothing; overwhelming majority of repeal's beneficiaries are heirs of people...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
NURSING SHORTAGE IS RAISING WORRIES ON PATIENTS' CARE
Nurses, hospital executives and health care experts warn that shortage of nurses across country is becoming so severe that it threatens patient care; vacancy rate for nursing positions at 73 hospitals in New York, Westchester and Long Island is averaging 8 percent, up sharply from 5.5 percent in 1999; shortage is even worse in California, where vacancies in 470 hospitals averaged 20 percent in December; because of short staffing, many nurses say they are forced to stay on job when they are exhausted after 12-hour shift; photo (L)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
CHINESE INSISTING U.S. MUST DO MORE TO END STANDOFF
China insists in strongly worded statement that United States has not done enough to end crisis over 24 Americans detained after their spy plane made emergency landing nearly week ago; negotiations to find way out of crisis continue; official propaganda in China both reflects popular anger and keeps it ratcheted high; China asserts that collision was 'entirely caused' by US side, and that US should apologize; photo; maps (L)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Darwin vs. Design: Evolutionists' New Battle
Evolutionists, who recently saw teaching of subject restored to Kansas schools, are now confronting new opponents, not biblical creationists but theory of 'intelligent design'; proponents accept that earth is billions of years old, not thousands suggested by literal reading of Bible, but say natural selection is not enough to explain complexity of plants and animals; proponents say they are open to non-biblical explanations, such as proposition that life was seeded by meteorite, extraterrestrial intelligence or new age notion of mysterious but inanimate life force; influence in several states noted; many of advocates have mainstream credentials, which could help make anti-evolutionism...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Sins of the Father, Visited Upon the Mother
UNTIL that terrible evening in May two years ago, Ekaete Udoh was both a battered wife and a good mother. But, she said, when her husband blackened the eye of their 11-year-old because the girl washed the dishes the wrong way, city officials labeled Mrs. Udoh a bad mother. She received a telephone call two days after the incident saying that her four daughters had been pulled out of school and put in a foster home. They stayed there three weeks.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
CHINA BUILDUP PUTS TAIWAN ON EDGE
Collision between American spy plane and Chinese fighter jet focuses attention on China's military buildup; but none of pilots at Chiayi Air Base in southern Taiwan need any reminders; with Chinese mainland just 100 miles away, Taiwan's F-16 fighter aircraft roar into air to patrol uneasy Taiwan strait; their Chinese counterparts, in turn, take to air in SU-27's and other Russian-designed fighters; China has steadily expanded its arsenal of missiles, aircraft, submarines and destroyers over last several years as its economy has grown; much of that buildup is aimed squarely at Taiwan; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Read All About It
ABOUT the only direct link between Manhattan and Johns Hopkins University Press is a humble deli serving New York-style corned beef on rye a few blocks from the publisher's offices in Baltimore. But Johns Hopkins, the nation's oldest university press, has ignored its Baltimore ZIP code and for the last three years has become an unlikely southern redoubt for books about New York. Its top seller is ''Invisible New York: The Hidden Infrastructure of the City,'' a lavish black-and-white photography book that features must-see landmarks like a Bronx missile silo and an abandoned Queens efflux, a chamber for the city's...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
QUOTATION OF THE DAY
''I'm an ordinary Chinese woman writing you this letter in tears from my sickbed.'' RUAN GUOQIN, wife of the missing Chinese pilot, in a letter to President Bush in which she complains about the lack of a United States apology. [10]
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
In a Colorful Campaign in Peru, 3 Points of View Emerge
Peruvians to vote for new president just year after election so clumsily fraudulent that winner, Alberto Fujimori, finally had to leave country and resign; Alejandro Toledo is likely to get most votes but not enough to avoid runoff, so that real race is between left-wing former president Alan Garcia and moderate conservative Lourdes Flores; photos (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Brontë Rapture, Far From the Moors
SLATE-GRAY clouds raced across the turbulent sky. A fine drizzle misted the landscape. Except for the buses trundling up the Avenue of the Americas, the ladies agreed as they peered out the wood-paneled windows, it was a perfect Bront&#235; day. They would know. American members of the Bront&#235; Society, they converged in New York last weekend for a day of communion with the three sisters whose peregrinations over the 19th-century English moors became the lodestone for their novels of romance, death and madness.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Day 2 · Mon, Apr 09, 2001
Titles10Wings Clipped, the Concorde Set Gets By; After Crash, Hope for Return to Days of the Quick Trans-Atlantic HopFor Levy, Can-Do Business Style Runs Into Education RealityMetro Matters; A Long View Of City Politics In Short TermCornell Will Open A Medical School In the Persian GulfOPPOSITION BUILDS TO SPENDING CUTS IN BUSH'S BUDGETClash of Cellular Towers and Hudson Valley VistasU.S. and China Look for a Way To Say 'Sorry'QUOTATION OF THE DAYMetropolitan DiaryBronx Journal; A Comeback Season for Bruising Irish Sports at Gaelic Park
Wings Clipped, the Concorde Set Gets By; After Crash, Hope for Return to Days of the Quick Trans-Atlantic Hop
Officials of both Air France and British Airways hope to have the Concorde flying again by this summer; Concorde, grounded since crash in Paris in July 2000, is sorely missed by many of the busy, the powerful and the very rich who flew it regularly and who have had to content themselves with subsonic flight for last seven months; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
For Levy, Can-Do Business Style Runs Into Education Reality
Schools Chancellor Harold O Levy's suffers some setbacks after 15 months in office; parents have rejected his proposal to privatize five failing schools, teachers' union has joined City Hall in ridiculing record size of his recent budget proposal, and Ninfa Segarra, longtime ally of Mayor Rudolph Giuliani and critics of Levy's managerial style, has been elected president of Board of Education; Levy remains optimistic, at least publicly, citing his success in starting fast-track program for certifying teachers, new performance standards for superintendents and shrinking of central board bureaucracy (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Metro Matters; A Long View Of City Politics In Short Term
Joyce Purnick Metro Matters interview with Councilwoman Ronnie M Eldridge, who cannot run for re-election because of term limits; she discusses her career, her support of term limits and her concerns for her Upper West Side of Manhattan district; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Cornell Will Open A Medical School In the Persian Gulf
Cornell University is establishing branch campus of its medical school in Middle East, where students will receive same diploma as those in New York City; Weill Medical College of Cornell University will open in Qatar, tiny, wealthy Persian Gulf nation that has agreed to spend $750 million on school over 11 years, including fee to Cornell; Cornell's agreement is far more comprehensive than programs abroad established by other American universities; Cornell will give full Ivy League medical degree to students who may never set foot in US; Cornell's president, Hunter R Rawlings III, says medical college in Qatar will have...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
OPPOSITION BUILDS TO SPENDING CUTS IN BUSH'S BUDGET
Lobbyists and their allies in Congress mobilize efforts to oppose spending cuts in Pres Bush's $1.96 trillion budget; Vice Pres Cheney warns Congress not to go too far in restoring spending, saying Bush would reject spending that he views as excessive; White House says cuts in domestic programs are needed to put federal spending on more sustainable path after several years of rapid growth; some members of Congress from both parties object that cuts are compelled mainly to pay for large tax cuts Bush is seeking; cahrt (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Clash of Cellular Towers and Hudson Valley Vistas
Opposition to new cellular telephone towers grows in Hudson Valley, NY; dozens of villages, towns and cities have been confronted with proposals for new towers between New York City and Albany that would jut 80 to 180 feet into sky; many of these governments are calling for moratoriums on tower building and are rushing to pass local laws to restrict size, shape and location of future monoliths; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U.S. and China Look for a Way To Say 'Sorry'
Chinese and American officials struggle to find sentences in English and Chinese that are close enough to be considered single statement about how Washington regards airplane incident yet distant enough to allow subtly different interpretations in each country; sticking point is China's public demand for apology; this reflects psychic thin skin that China's Communist Party has nurtured among people, so that any slight to national dignity is acutely felt by all; it also reflects growing apprehension in China about US's role as world's sole superpower, impervious to others and free to do as it will; Pres Bush and Seretary of...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
QUOTATION OF THE DAY
''He's not like anyone we've seen before in the game.'' MARK CALCAVECCHIA, on Tiger Woods. [D6]
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Metropolitan Diary
DEAR DIARY: I live in Richmond, Va., and was visiting New York on St. Patrick's Day when a contingent of firemen passed by in the parade. Suddenly, one fireman broke rank, ran to the side of Fifth Avenue and 84th Street, knelt on the pavement and proposed marriage to his girlfriend. She said yes, grabbed his face and gave him a huge smooch as the crowd applauded. The fireman then took out an engagement ring and slipped it on her finger. She gave him another huge smooch and the crowd went wild with delight. He then lifted her over the...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Bronx Journal; A Comeback Season for Bruising Irish Sports at Gaelic Park
Article describes scene at Gaelic Park in Bronx during scrimmage between two New York hurling teams; photo (M)

Titles10Coming Soon: The Wax Menagerie; Tussaud's Is Giving Times Square Something New to Gawk AtIn New Jersey Profiling Case, Debate Centers on Driver's StatementGenetics Offers Denouement To Mystery of Prince's DeathPoverty Found To Be Rising In Families Considered SafeCUBAN BOY STAYS IN U.S. FOR NOW, A COURT DECIDESLouisiana Trial Dissects Image Of Ex-Governor2 Fiber Studies Find No Benefit For the ColonFor South African Whites, Money Has No ColorContract Accord Averts Strike by DoormenMiranda Decision Has Its Day in Court
Coming Soon: The Wax Menagerie; Tussaud's Is Giving Times Square Something New to Gawk At
Article discusses $2.5 million publicity campaign for fall opening of Madame Tussaud's New York, wax museum that will be located in Times Square; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
In New Jersey Profiling Case, Debate Centers on Driver's Statement
Article contends that in two years since two New Jersey State Police officers wounded three unarmed minority men in van on New Jersey Turnpike, incident has ignited furious, politically charged policy debate about how to prevent troopers from singling out black and Latino drivers; but as case against Troopers John Hogan and James Kenna advances, defense lawyers have used van driver Keshon Moore's own words to argue whatever discrimination problems may afflict state police force, shooting was justified because troopers believed their lives were in danger when van accelerated toward them; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Genetics Offers Denouement To Mystery of Prince's Death
Two scientists say they have proof that disease-riddled boy who died in 1795 after years alone in prison cell in France really was Louis XVII, son of Louis XVI and Marie Antoinette; more than 500 books have been written about young dauphin, and many theories have emerged about his possible escape and presumed exile after his parents were sent to guillotine at height of French Revolution; would-be heirs have popped up all over Europe through the years; scientists, Jean-Jacques Cassiman and Bernd Brinkmann, both specialists in human genetics, examined remains of boy--a dried heart--and claim they matched his DNA with...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Poverty Found To Be Rising In Families Considered Safe
New analysis of Census Data released by Community Service Society shows that in New York City, despite economic boom, poverty rates rose sharply for two-person family with children, with one parent working; study finds that poverty rate for such families climbed to 32.3 percent from 29.3 percent in 1998; graph (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
CUBAN BOY STAYS IN U.S. FOR NOW, A COURT DECIDES
Federal appeals court in Atlanta extends order that will keep Elian Gonzalez in United States until court hears full appeal of his case on May 11, and suggests that efforts of boy's Miami relatives' to have his request for asylum heard in court should not have been ignored; court's language seems to chastise government for asserting that only boy's father is legally entitled to speak for him; ruling ensures that questions of who rightfully speaks for boy and whether he is entitled to apply for asylum will be fully aired; ruling does not answer key question: who should have custody...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Louisiana Trial Dissects Image Of Ex-Governor
Federal prosecutors give closing arguments in four-month trial of Edwin W Edwards, former governor of Louisiana, portraying him as relentlessly, reflexively, almost pathologically corrupt; case involves five casino licensing extortion schemes; unlike Edwards's previous trials, one of which ended in mistrial and other in acquittal, Edwards in this case has faced hundreds of hours of audiotapes produced by clandestine wiretaps; prosecutors also had benefit of extensive testimony from three former business associates of Edwards's, including Edward J DeBartolo Jr, former owner of San Francisco 49ers; they all turned state's evidence against Edwards as part of their plea bargains with government...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
2 Fiber Studies Find No Benefit For the Colon
Two rigorous studies cast doubt on benefit of eating low-fat, high-fiber foods to reduce risk of colon and rectal cancer; studies find no difference in colon cancer risk among those following, and not following, such a diet; experts on colon cancer are stunned at results of research, led by scientists at National Cancer Institute and University of Arizona; colon and rectal cancer is second-leading cause of cancer-related death in United States; some researchers say it is time to stop telling people that low-fat, high-fiber diet will protect them; public health experts say fiber can have other beneficial results in diet...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
For South African Whites, Money Has No Color
Article on growing confidence and increased prosperity of wealthy Afrikaners under black rule in South Africa; Afrikaners, descendants of 17th-century Dutch, German and French settlers, remained in South Africa when government finally changed hands; by contrast, many English-speaking descendants of British colonialists, who controlled much of economy under apartheid, did leave, enabling Afrikaners to fill gaps; between 1996 and 1999, Afrikaner businessmen have seen their share of Johannesburg Stock Exchange surge from 24 percent to 36 percent as they snap up companies shed by emigrating firms and begin dominating growing technology and electronics sectors; Afrikaner businessmen who once viewed black...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Contract Accord Averts Strike by Doormen
Local 32B-32J of Service Employees International Union, which represents workers at 2,900 New York City apartment buildings, reaches tentative agreement with real estate industry, averting strike threatened for tomorrow; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Miranda Decision Has Its Day in Court
Supreme Court hears arguments for and against upholding Miranda warning that requires police to inform suspects of their right to remain silent and to consult lawyer; 34-year-old precedent is landmark of Warren Court; far more conservative Rehnquist Court is considering constitutionality of long-ignored Section 3501 of Omnibus Crime Control and Safe Streets Act of 1968, which provides that Miranda warnings are not necessary and that federal trial judges could decide whether confession was voluntary; Solicitor Gen Seth P Waxman argues for Clinton administration in support of Miranda; Justices Sandra Day O'Connor and Anthony M Kennedy, who are widely perceived as...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Day 2 · Fri, Apr 21, 2000
Titles10Perfection Made Easy; Bowling a 300 Game Just Isn't the Feat It Used to Be2 With Ties to Chief of Welfare Got Jobs With Major ContractorU.S. Gathers Officers, Preparing To Take Cuban From Miami KinStates Declare War On Divorce Rates, Before Any 'I Dos'Scientists Say They Have Found the Heart of a DinosaurIn Illinois, China Trade Bill Pulls Congressman 2 WaysMEDICARE SPENDING FOR CARE AT HOME PLUNGES BY 45%Bronx Museum Asks Jurors to Do Lunch, ArtfullyCities Failing To Heed Call Of the CensusA City Bedazzling Generations
Perfection Made Easy; Bowling a 300 Game Just Isn't the Feat It Used to Be
Increasing number of bowlers are throwing perfect games largely because of advances in bowling-ball technology; American Bowling Congress says there were 905 perfect games in 1968-69 season and 34,470 in 1998-99 season; increase comes at time when number of people who bowl regularly has declined drastically; photos (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
2 With Ties to Chief of Welfare Got Jobs With Major Contractor
Officials with Maximus, nation's largest operator of welfare-to-work programs for state and local governments, deny getting inside information about other companies bidding for lucrative contracts to help New York City welfare recipients find jobs; other bidders say Tony A Kearney, who has close ties to New York City's welfare commissioner, Jason A Turner, was put on Maximus's payroll, at Turner's request, and allowed to gather information about operations of companies competing with Maximus; note that Turner's father-in-law, Daniel J Dornbrook, also works for Maximus; disclosures are latest in series of problems with city's bidding process on Mayor Rudolph W Giuliani's...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U.S. Gathers Officers, Preparing To Take Cuban From Miami Kin
Atty Gen Janet Reno reportedly orders federal law-enforcement authorities to plan for removal of Elian Gonzalez from home of his Miami relatives, forcibly if necessary, and to return him to his father; relatives have refused to hand over boy, and government officials say time has nearly run out on negotiated solution; say law-enforcement action will be carried out by immigration agents and federal marshals; Pres Clinton tells reporters that father should wait no longer for his son; hardening rhetoric and surge of behind-the-scenes activity come day after three-judge panel of United States Court of Appeals in Atlanta bars authorities from...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
States Declare War On Divorce Rates, Before Any 'I Dos'
Growing number of states require or encourage engaged couples to take marital education course out of concern about high social and economic costs of divorce; other initiatives include Wisconsin's hiring of 'marriage czar' to urge clergy to set requirements for engaged couples, and Arkansas's exploration of tax credit for couples taking marital course; Utah includes marital skills in high school curriculums, and Florida makes marital course high school graduation requirement; critics of such initiatives say government is meddling in marriage; close to half of marriages end in divorce; photos (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Scientists Say They Have Found the Heart of a Dinosaur
Paleontologists say they found heart of dinosaur in chest cavity of dinosaur's fossil skeleton uncovered in South Dakota, and that organ was more like heart of bird or mammal than any known reptile; finding could profoundly change theories of dinosaur physiology and place of at least some of those creatures in evolutionary history of life; scientists describe heart as size of grapefruit, fossilized into reddish-brown stone 66 million years ago; Dr Dale A Russell of North Carolina Museum of Natural Sciences, announcing discovery, says evidence for four-chambered heart with single aorta strongly suggests that this and perhaps many other dinosaurs...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
In Illinois, China Trade Bill Pulls Congressman 2 Ways
Landmark legislation to normalize trade relations with China pulls Rep David Phelps, freshman Illinois Democrat, in opposite directions by diverse constituency in his sprawling district; farmers and agribusiness giants urge him to vote yes to help open rich new Chinese markets to American goods; longtime labor allies lobby hard against deal, saying it would cost hundreds of union jobs and reward repressive Communist government that has cheated on other trade pacts; Phelps and 39 other undecided House Democrats will probably control bill's fate; his silence on how he will vote has catapulted him into limelight; decision by House Democratic leader...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
MEDICARE SPENDING FOR CARE AT HOME PLUNGES BY 45%
Federal payments to provide home health care for elderly dropped 45 percent in last two years, forcing many Medicare patients to spend more time in hospitals and nursing homes; sharp decline in spending stems from changes Congress adopted in 1997 to slow growth of Medicare; trend has surprised and alarmed advocates for elderly, health policy experts and many members of Congress; Sen Susan Collins claims Medicare home health cutbacks have been far deeper and more wide-reaching than Congress intended; Congressional Budget Office figures, showing sharp drop in Medicare spending, do not show how much payments to hospitals and nursing homes...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Bronx Museum Asks Jurors to Do Lunch, Artfully
Bronx Museum of Arts offers free meal and guided tour to prospective jurors at nearby Bronx Supreme Court in hope that they will come back again; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Cities Failing To Heed Call Of the Census
Census Bureau figures show that mail responses to census ranked among lowest in nation in Hartford, Conn, and Newark, NJ, cities with large pockets of poor residents who do not speak English fluently and who may not trust authorities; only 42 percent of Hartford's households mailed in census forms, 1 percentage point below April 1990 rate; return rate in Newark stands at 43 percent, also 1 percentage point below April 1990 rate (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
A City Bedazzling Generations
Clyde Haberman column notes that letter from Emily Ryals, 14-year-old girl from Keller, Texas, to her great-great-aunt Nora Miller Turman about her 1999 visit to New York City and Turman's 1929 letter to her mother on her visit to New York show how much and yet how little city has changed over decades (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
