Day 1 · Thu, Apr 01, 1999
Titles104 Officers Enter Not-Guilty Pleas To Murder Counts in Diallo Case3 G.I.'s Missing in Macedonia After They Reported AttackNATO PLANS WEEKS OF BOMBING TO BREAK GRIP OF SERB LEADERTwo Rivals in Clerical Union Are Charged in EmbezzlementMany Hospitals in New York Quit Plan for Fewer DoctorsNATO Had Signs Its Strategy Would Fail KosovarsOur Towns; Losing a Battle To Rattles And FangsIn Grim Week, Pep Talk From the PresidentSerbs Shelling Wide Swath of KosovoOne Year, Old Lament: No Budget
4 Officers Enter Not-Guilty Pleas To Murder Counts in Diallo Case
New York City Police Officers Sean Carroll, Edward McMellon, Richard Murphy and Kenneth Boss are arraigned on charges of second-degree murder and suspended in shooting death of Amadou Diallo; they plead not guilty; State Supreme Court Judge John P Collins rejects Bronx Dist Atty Robert T Johnson's request to put officers in custody, and sets bail at $100,000 each; hundreds of people on both sides of case demonstrate outside Bronx County Building; security is so heavy for arraignment that building is shut down; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
3 G.I.'s Missing in Macedonia After They Reported Attack
Pentagon says three American infantrymen patrolling Macedonia's border with Kosovo are missing after reporting by radio that they had been surrounded and were under fire; say soldiers, whose names are not released, might have been captured by Serbian forces; CNN broadcasts videotape from Serbian television showing what Serbs identify as three Americans who were reported missing on March 31; soldiers are part of US First Infantry Division, which had about 350 members in Macedonia for years, remnant of United Nations force that is being withdrawn now that thousands of NATO troops are stationed near border; map (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
NATO PLANS WEEKS OF BOMBING TO BREAK GRIP OF SERB LEADER
NATO opens its expanded bombing campaign against Yugoslavia; officials are planning weeks of air strikes intended to undermine President Slobodan Milosevic's hold on power; say stepped-up bombing of broader range of sites as second week of air strikes begins is intended to give alliance more effective tool to press Milosevic to halt Serbian onslaught in Kosovo; bombing of Yugoslavia has had opposite effect so far; Serbs, including some who oppose Milosevic, have united behind him; map; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Two Rivals in Clerical Union Are Charged in Embezzlement
Francine Autovino and Connie Lango, former president and former treasurer of union local representing City University clerical workers, are indicted on charges of embezzlement in connection with disappearance of more than $240,000; charges are first indictments brought by Manhattan District Attorney Robert M Morgenthau in his lengthy investigation of corruption at District Council 37; three other union leaders in District Council 37 previously pleaded guilty in case (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Many Hospitals in New York Quit Plan for Fewer Doctors
Dozens of teaching hospitals in New York State in 1997 embraced unusual pilot program under which Federal Government agreed to pay bonuses to hospitals if they trained fewer doctors; half those hospitals have dropped out of plan after finding that they cannot function without low-cost labor provided by doctors in training; development, which comes even as Government is planning to expand New York program nationwide, raises doubts about Federal efforts to curb number of doctors in nation; pilot program in New York was devised by Greater New York Hospital Association officials; it was approved by Health Care Financing Administration, Federal...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
NATO Had Signs Its Strategy Would Fail Kosovars
Top civilian and military leaders of NATO settled on their strategy against Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic despite several military assessments and intelligence warnings, and even clue from Yugoslav Gen Momcilo Perisic, that bombs alone could not stop Serb forces from carrying out purge in Kosovo; finger-pointing about missed signals and suggestions of mismanagement begin to surface as second week of bombing campaign begins with no sign that Milosevic is buckling, and no idea how it will end; Pentagon planners say they warned Clinton Administration that Milosevic was likely to strike out against Kosovo Albanians as soon as possibility of military...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Our Towns; Losing a Battle To Rattles And Fangs
Andrew C Revkin Our Towns column on plans by Jay Montfort to appeal decision by Dutchess County, NY, Supreme Court Judge Judith A Hillery that fence he built surrounding Sour Mountain Realty, his mining company in Fishkill, NY, to keep snakes off his property must be taken down; New York State environmental officials argued that venemous timber rattlesnakes, which are found in state park next to Montfort's mining operation and are on list of endangered species, might be harmed by fence; they have ruled that granite mining operations cannot proceed without new study on possible effects on snakes (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
In Grim Week, Pep Talk From the President
Pres Clinton has attempted to present picture of confidence as reports of atrocities and pictures of columns of refugees pour in from Balkans; during meeting with his top foreign policy officials on March 29, after Balkans went through wrenching weekend, he decided they needed pep talk; it is telling that only four days into long-planned bombardment of Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic's forces, Clinton needed to buck up senior officials who advised him to pursue military option; to do so, he had to conceal his own doubts about course he had chosen (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Serbs Shelling Wide Swath of Kosovo
Refugees arriving in Albania say Serbian forces are shelling villages, towns and cities in Kosovo; say Pristina, Kosovo capital, is in flames, with widespread death and destruction; Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe says shelling has driven more than 10,000 ethnic Albanians from their homes; says refugees crossed border at Morini, joining 84,600 other refugees who have fled into Albania since crossing was opened on March 27, after being sealed for 11 months; map; photos (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
One Year, Old Lament: No Budget
New York State, for 15th consecutive year, finishes its fiscal year without a budget in place, event now so routine that, instead of outrage it once engendered, is met in Capitol with quiet resignation; lawmakers, on Passover and Easter break, have not convened single budget conference committee meeting and legislative leaders have not met with Gov George Pataki for single budget negotiation; many legislators are predicting that this year will bring a return to protracted battles of 1994 through 1997 (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Day 2 · Fri, Apr 02, 1999
Titles10Court Authorizes the State Audits That Giuliani Had Tried to BlockALLIES HIT TROOPS AND BRIDGE; SERBS MAY TRY 3 CAPTURED G.I.'SWest Talks of Plan to Rebuild Kosovo as Protected EnclaveWild (and Unleashed) Coyote Is Captured in Central ParkIllegal Immigrant Workers Being Fired in I.N.S. TacticIn Surprise Move, Gotti Asks to Be Returned to JailIn Diallo Case, All Sides Seek Answers in ScienceRefugees Tell of Methodical Emptying of PristinaMilitary Court Will Examine Soldiers' CasesLottery Hooks Gamblers, And Albany
Court Authorizes the State Audits That Giuliani Had Tried to Block
New York State appeals court rules Mayor Rudolph Giuliani exceeded his authority when he blocked state auditors from reviewing how effectively New York City's government is managed; unanimous decision upholds lawsuit by State Comptroller H Carl McCall that sought to compel city auditors to cooperate with his probe of how well city delivers services; says State Constitution and state laws cleary give Comptroller authority to conduct performance audits; McCall says he will pursue audits; Giuliani says he will make available most, if not all, of information sought by Comptroller; court decision settles bitter dispute between two of state's senior elected...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
ALLIES HIT TROOPS AND BRIDGE; SERBS MAY TRY 3 CAPTURED G.I.'S
NATO forces destroy bridge over Danube River in Serbia and attack Serbian mechanized column involved in purges in Kosovo province; bridge in city of Novi Sad is first target in NATO's campaign that could disrupt lives of ordinary Serbs, not just armed forces; officials insist pace and intensity of air raids is unrelated to capture of three American soldiers and their display on Yugoslav television; US reacts angrily to Yugoslav reports that soldiers may be put on trial; says such a move would be violation of international law; Pentagon says soldiers, seized while patrolling Yugoslav-Macedonia border, should be considered prisoners...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
West Talks of Plan to Rebuild Kosovo as Protected Enclave
Western officials weigh plan under which NATO troops would escort ethnic Albanians back to Kosovo after Serbian forces are routed by bombing, and would set up self-governing enclave under allied protection; say ground troops may be called in to bring back refugees and set up 'protectorate,' if air strikes succeed in quashing Serb military; say move might be possible even if formal peace agreement with Belgrade has not been reached, as long as it is clear that Serbs could not mount serious resistance (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Wild (and Unleashed) Coyote Is Captured in Central Park
Police capture coyote in Central Park and take him to Bronx Zoo; parks officials say coyote will eventually be released in wilds upstate; say that coyotes have become increasing presence in New York City, particularly in Bronx; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Illegal Immigrant Workers Being Fired in I.N.S. Tactic
Immigration and Naturalization Service begins stepped-up crackdown against illegal immigrant workers, using strategy that audits workers' documentation in industries nationwide; auditing approach, less expensive and cumbersome that deportation of illegals found in workplace, is effort to cut them off from work--goal that brought them to country in first place; policy is creating wrenching dilemmas for workers, many of whom have quietly held jobs for years, settled in homes and have children born in US; has created widespread resentment in many places, such as Yakima (Wash), and not only among workers but also among employers, who are wondering how to deal...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
In Surprise Move, Gotti Asks to Be Returned to Jail
John A Gotti, who won hard-fought battle to be released on bail six months ago, asks Judge Barrington D Parker Jr to put him back behind bars; move jars what had been routine hearing about jury selection procedures on eve of Gotti's trial on Federal racketeering charges; he had to persuade friends and relatives to put up their homes and businesses to secure his $10 million bail; Gotti was released on bail under carefully monitored conditions, and has to pay about $24,000 a month for security guards; Parker grants his request (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
In Diallo Case, All Sides Seek Answers in Science
Battle is already taking shape over physical evidence in Amadou Diallo case, as lawyers and experts seek to buttress their own versions of what happened based on entrance wounds, bullet trajectories and other forensic details; since there were apparently no eyewitnesses to shooting, such evidence could be crucial element when Police Officers Sean Carroll, Edward McMellon, Richard Murphy and Kenneth Boss are tried on murder charges; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Refugees Tell of Methodical Emptying of Pristina
Refugees arriving in Kukes, Albania, describe systematic emptying-out of Pristina, Kosovo's capital city by masked and heavily armed Serb gunmen; describe sudden eviction from homes, forced march through streets to railroad station and soccer stadium, and subsequent ride on packed trains, buses and trucks to areas near Macedonian and Albanian borders; some say they were robbed of money by Serbs; photos (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Military Court Will Examine Soldiers' Cases
Three US soldiers captured along Yugoslav-Macedonian border will appear before Yugoslav military court; US says court-martial would be violation of international law; Staff Sgt Andrew A Ramirez, Staff Sgt Christopher J Stone and Specialist Steven M Gonzales are paraded on Serbian state television, which also broadcasts unexpected meeting between Pres Slobodan Milosevic and Ibrahim Rugova, prominent leader of Kosovo's ethnic Albanians and part of delegation that signed Western-drafted peace agreement in Rambouillet, France, that Serbs rejected; both leaders sign document calling for peaceful end to Kosovo crisis through political means; spokesman for ethnic Albanian rebels calls Rugova's meeting high treason...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Lottery Hooks Gamblers, And Albany
Clyde Haberman column notes that Quick Draw lottery, which takes in $120 million or more a year, got caught up in annual state budget battle and was not renewed beyond its March 31 expiration; cites contention of experts on gambling addiction that lottery, with its rapid play on television monitors and new game every few minutes in thousands of bars and delis, has left more than few people hooked on gambling (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search

Titles10Cooperation and Miscalculations On Shaping Tobacco LegislationBarricade-Weary Pedestrians Welcome New Midblock CrosswalksAbout New York; Missing a Man Who Tapped Their DreamsSigns of Abuse Escape Notice Of an AgencyCasino in Atlantic City Loses Gehry as Principal ArchitectIn New York, Irish Accord Prompts SkepticismQUOTATION OF THE DAYJudge Orders City Hall to Let Reporters Review Welfare RecordsU.S. Says North Korea Helped Develop New Pakistani MissilePalestinians and Hamas Feud Over Killing of a Bomb Maker
Cooperation and Miscalculations On Shaping Tobacco Legislation
Article on months of haggling between Clinton Administration and Republican Senate in shaping anti-tobacco bill; negotiations since January 15 are partly tale of recalculations of industry's political power by Administration and Senate, and manufacturers' miscalculations of their own power and of whom they needed to appease; while few people doubt legislation is only one step in process that has uncertain ending, bill is framing escalating debate over nation's tobacco policy, and has become target of tobacco industry's anger; five largest companies have announced that they will not longer work for comprehensive legislation to regulate tobacco, and will vigorously fight Senate...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Barricade-Weary Pedestrians Welcome New Midblock Crosswalks
New York City installs traffic signals and crosswalks in middle of two blocks, steps from where it has erected barriers to keep pedestrians from blocking flow of turning cars; pedestrians hail move, latest phase in shifting struggle for control of midtown's busiest streets; photos (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
About New York; Missing a Man Who Tapped Their Dreams
David Gonzalez About New York tribute to Richard Ellner, amateur dancer and businessman who ran Manhattan dance studio; Ellner died recently at age 69; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Signs of Abuse Escape Notice Of an Agency
Confidential New York City report on 1997 murder of Daytwon Bennett, 5, holds caseworker who checked on abusive mother regularly after children were returned to her from foster care never looked for signs of danger; holds she never failed to talk with him alone, check him for bruises or notice he was starving; report offers rare glimpse at challenges facing Children's Services Administration two years after promised reforms; mother, Jocelyn Bennett, awaits trial (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Casino in Atlantic City Loses Gehry as Principal Architect
Frank O Gehry says he will not be main architect of $2 billion hotel-casino project Mirage Resorts plans for Atlantic City; leading architect, Philip Johnson, will continue to work with Mirage's own architects (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
In New York, Irish Accord Prompts Skepticism
Irish and Irish-Americans New Yorkers express skepticism about Northern Ireland peace accord (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
QUOTATION OF THE DAY
''It doesn't take courage to shoot a policeman in the back of the head, or to murder an unarmed taxi driver. What takes courage is to compete in the arena of democracy as these men and women are tonight.'' GEORGE J. MITCHELL, who headed the Northern Ireland peace talks. [A1]
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Judge Orders City Hall to Let Reporters Review Welfare Records
Justice Bernard J Fried orders New York City to give reporters access to names and addresses of people who have left welfare; New York Times, which brougth suit, plans random interviews with many former recipients to see if they have gotten jobs and how they are managing; city plans appeal (S)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U.S. Says North Korea Helped Develop New Pakistani Missile
US officials and private analysts say Pakistan's test of new liquid-fueled missile able to strike deep inside India is based on technology smuggled from North Korea; say weapon is enhanced version of North Korean Rodong missile, which is advanced version of Scud missile developed in Moscow in 1960's (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Palestinians and Hamas Feud Over Killing of a Bomb Maker
Palestinian Authority and Hamas meet to defuse growing feud over who killed Muhyiaddin al-Sharif, Hamas bomb-maker wanted by Israel for several terrorist attacks who was found murdered in Ramallah; Palestinian Authority accuses expatriate leaders of Hamas of trying to provoke confrontation by pushing internal leaders to break longstanding agreement not to criticize Palestinian leadership in public; accuses Hamas of killing Sharif in internal feud out of suspicion he was collaborating with Israel (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Day 2 · Sun, Apr 12, 1998
Titles10Can-Do American's Patience Paid Off With an Ulster PactPolitical Interests Arouse Raging Debate on CensusWhose Building Is It Anyway?Old Places, New FacesQUOTATION OF THE DAYF.Y.I.Japan's Blossoms Soothe a P.O.W. Lost in SiberiaMAKING IT WORK; StackedRancor Eases As the Koreas Open Talks After 4 YearsItalian Fashion House Offers Exotic Sportswear
Can-Do American's Patience Paid Off With an Ulster Pact
George J Mitchell gave up comforts of retirement from United States Senate to spend three years trying to wring peace agreement out of feuding political leaders of Northern Ireland; all sides are praising him for his stewardship during 22 months of talks it took to produce peace accord; there is general agreement that without his guidance and forbearance no accord would have emergered; Mitchell says he was motivated by belief that children of Northern Ireland deserve same peace and political stability that his children enjoy; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Political Interests Arouse Raging Debate on Census
Census planned for year 2000 is generating firestorm of debate, strategizing in Congress and litigation aimed at blocking Census Bureau from changing way it does business; at issue is plan to substitute statistical sampling method for traditional method of trying to count every person in country; only all people in 90 percent of households in each census tract, comprising about 1,700 dwellings, will be counted with statisticians making subsequent adjustments to final total; Republicans say statistical sampling violates Constitution, which calls for actual enumeration every 10 years, and is much too complex for Census Bureau to perform successfully; Democrats staunchly...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Whose Building Is It Anyway?
Coping column on continuing warfare in Housing Court between tenants at 230 East 167th Street in the Bronx and building's owners, Stuart Morgan and Steven Finkelstein; Geovanny David is fighting eviction for nonpayment of rent (S)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Old Places, New Faces
Article on rapid socioeconomic changes in southern Brooklyn as blacks move into traditionally white neighborhoods; population shifts also involve Hispanics and Asian immmigrants, and Orthodox Jews who are crowded or priced out of Midwood and are, unusually, 'turning' blocks from black to white; blacks and Orthodox Jews coexist amicably in neighborhoods less scrutinized than Crown Heights, while Italians in Bay Ridge puzzle over origin of dark-skinned newcomers, and join move to Staten Island and suburbs; photos; map; Russians, now 6.7 percent of borough's population and eager to Americanize, are moving to homes and opening businesses beyond boundaries of Brighton Beach...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
QUOTATION OF THE DAY
''We don't learn anything here. The program says you come here for training, but all we do is pick up paper.'' EUSTACIA OJEDA, a workfare worker at the Parade Grounds in Brooklyn. [30]
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
F.Y.I.
New York City Q&A column on office building log books; old Eberhard Faber Pencil Company building on Greenpoint Avenue in Brooklyn; Bartholdi statue of Lafayette in Union Square
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Japan's Blossoms Soothe a P.O.W. Lost in Siberia
Interview with 77-year-old Toshimasa Meguro, Japanese prisoner of war who is returning home after 53 years in Siberian prison camp where he and other Japanese soldiers, who were captured by Russians during World War II, were sent and subsequently forgotten; by some accounts, dozens of Japanese soldiers are still alive in Russia; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
MAKING IT WORK; Stacked
Photos of some tall old smokestacks on waterfront sites in New York City
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Rancor Eases As the Koreas Open Talks After 4 Years
North and South Korean officials meet in Beijing in first face-to-face talks in more than four years; friendly discussion covers broad range of issues, ranging from large-scale economic investment to plans for reuniting families separated by division (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Italian Fashion House Offers Exotic Sportswear
New Iceberg sportswear shop on Madison Avenue noted (New York retailing column) (S)

Day 1 · Tue, Apr 22, 1997
Titles10Flooding Crests in Ravaged City; Residents Face Weeks of AnxietyThe Hard Times of a Coal Town, Ignored in Booming Britain's VoteFor the Nation's Politicians, Big Tobacco No Longer Bites4th Body Found in Fire; City Says House Had Illegal UnitsCLINTON APPROVES NEW U.S. SANCTIONS AGAINST BURMESETeen-Agers Are Arrested In Killing of Pizza MenA Final Turn-On Lifts Timothy Leary OffAt Fordham, War of Words On Party Site2 Held in Thrill-Killings of Pizza MenJury Complete in Megan Case
Flooding Crests in Ravaged City; Residents Face Weeks of Anxiety
Red River crests at record 54 feet, or nearly twice flood stage, in Grand Forks, ND, causing widespread flooding and forcing residents out of homes for at least three weeks; most of 50,000 population will face crushing losses, as approximattely 90 percent of city is inundated; Pres Clinton will visit ravaged area, where virtually all business has come to halt; widespread damage described; photos; map (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
The Hard Times of a Coal Town, Ignored in Booming Britain's Vote
Ghettos of reinvigorated urban centers and downtrodden rural villages near affluent towns all over Britain are all but forgotten by both Tories and Labor in current general election campaign; residents of rural areas such as Grimethorpe, South Yorkshire mining town, find themselves neglected and invisible, and left behind in recovering economy; are resentful of Tories for shutting down coal pits, but are no longer enamored of Labor Party, to which they have long been loyal, since party has refashioned itself to appeal to voters of prosperous Middle England, none of whom exist in Grimethorpe, which remains poor with abundance of...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
For the Nation's Politicians, Big Tobacco No Longer Bites
Focus on rapid shift in public sentiment against tobacco companies, and how change has emboldened political opposition to industry, particularly at state and local level; while legal and financial considerations may have spurred tobacco executives to seek negotiated settlement of class-action suits, they have also been pushed along by rapid changes in relationship between politics and tobacco; political support for industry has crumbled rapidly in last three years in response to steady stream of disclosures about deceptive marketing practices; erosion began in 1980's with new awareness of dangers of second-hand smoke, and passage of ordinances in several cities prohibiting smoking...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
4th Body Found in Fire; City Says House Had Illegal Units
Death toll in fire that destroyed house in Maspeth, Queens, climbs to four and city officials say building had been illegally converted to provide cheap sleeping quarters to struggling immigrants; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
CLINTON APPROVES NEW U.S. SANCTIONS AGAINST BURMESE
Pres Clinton, after weeks of internal debate, approves ban on new American investment in Myanmar because of human-rights abuses by military Government; acts under terms of law signed in 1996 that mandates sanctions if Burmese military commits large-scale repression against democratic opposition, or if it again detains opposition leader, Daw Aung San Suu Kyi, who has been back under house arrest since November 1996 (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Teen-Agers Are Arrested In Killing of Pizza Men
Two small-town New Jersey teen-agers were arrested yesterday and charged with what officials called the brutal, senseless and elaborately plotted murders of two pizza deliverymen, who were lured to an abandoned house in a remote area of Sussex County on Saturday night and shot to death in their car. The victims appeared to have been chosen randomly, their wallets and cash untouched. Officials said the slayers' motive may have been the thrill of killing.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
A Final Turn-On Lifts Timothy Leary Off
Ashes of Dr Timothy Leary, LSD prophet of 1960's, and those of 23 others are launched into Earth orbit aboard Spain's first space satellite; remains, taken aloft in American Pegasus rocket launched from Lockheed L-1011 airplane off Canary Islands, will orbit every 90 minutes for perhaps two years, or as many as 10; include those of Gene Roddenberry, who created Star Trek television series, and space physicist, Gerard O'Neill; arrangements for first funeral in space were made by Celestis Inc of Houston; photos; maps (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
At Fordham, War of Words On Party Site
Clyde Haberman NYC column discusses dispute on campus of Fordham University over selection of Intrepid, decommissioned aircraft carrier of World War II vintage, as site of Senior Ball (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
2 Held in Thrill-Killings of Pizza Men
Two Sussex County, NJ, teenagers, identified as 17-year-old Jay Vreeland and 18-year-old Thomas J Koskovich, are arrested and charged with murders of two pizza deliverymen, who were lured into abandoned house in Franklin and shot to death in car; police describe murders as elaborately plotted thrill-killing in which victims were randomly chosen; say defendants called four pizza parlors before finding one willing to deliver to isolated spot in Franklin, where they were waiting in dark; victims identified as 24-year-old Georgio Gallara, owner of Tony's Pizza and Pasta Restaurant, in Hamburg, NJ, and 22-year-old employee Jeremy Giordano; photos; map (M)

Jury Complete in Megan Case
Jury selection is completed in trial of Jesse Timmendequas for murder-rape of 7-year-old Megan Kanka in 1994 (New Jersey Daily Briefing) (S)

Officials Say China Illegally Sent U.S. Equipment to Military Plant
Intelligence officials say Federal criminal inquiry has uncovered new evidence suggesting that state-owned Chinese company, Catic, had intended all along to divert American machine equipment to military plant that builds missiles and fighter aircraft; equipment was supposed to be used solely for civilian purposes; Clinton Administration is faced with question of how to proceed if it is proved that Catic knowingly misled US officials; Administration approved Catic's purchase of machine equipment from McDonnell Douglas Corp in Aug 1994; officials say some equipment sent from US wound up at military complex in Nanchang (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Whitewater Counsel Says He Has Evidence Of Obstructing Justice
Whitewater prosecutor, Kenneth W Starr, says he has found 'extensive evidence' of obstruction of justice and needs more time to complete his investigation; asks for six-month extension of grand jury that is investigating political and personal affairs of Pres Clinton and his wife, Hillary; Judge Susan Webber Wright grants request, extending life of grand jury until Nov 7; Starr says his investigators are evaluating new information obtained from James B McDougal; recently began to investigate matter of lucrative payments in 1994 to Webster L Hubbell, former Justice Department official and a crucial witness in several of the aspects of Starr's...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Conservative Senators Pressure G.O.P. Leaders in Budget Talks
Ten conservative Republican Senators issue list of budget demands, warning majority leader Trent Lott that they will not support any deal that does not meet their conditions; among conditions is hold on domestic spending, 'meaningful' tax relief and no adjustment to Consumer Price Index; House Speaker Newt Gingrich says he has grown impatient over balanced budget talks and is ready to act; Sens who signed letter to Lott are Phil Gramm, Sam Brownback, John Ashcroft, Larry E Craig, Lauch Faircloth, Jesse Helms, James M Inhofe, Rick Santorum, Richard C Shelby and Robert C Smith (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
PERU TROOPS RESCUE HOSTAGES; REBELS SLAIN AS STANDOFF ENDS
Peruvian troops storm Japanese Ambassador's residence in Lima and rescue scores of captives from Tupac Amaru Revolutionary Movement guerrillas who had held them hostage for four months; Pres Alberto K Fujimori says all but one of 72 hostages were rescued and 14 of rebels were killed, including leader, Nestor Cerpa Cartolini; hostage who died was Supreme Court Justice Carlos Giusti Acuna; Prime Min Ryutaro Hashimoto of Japan says his country was not informed in advance of attack; Fujimori had agreed to consult Japanese before taking any military action; map; photos (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Killing of 2 Deliverymen Brings Unwanted Attention
You pass the Dunkin' Donuts on Route 23 and turn right at the Franklin Diner. You follow the dips and rises of the road as it winds past a stream good for fishing and rows of houses spruced with blue wildflowers. Then you turn right onto Scott Road, go past a well-kept horse farm, and there it is: the abandoned house with yellow trim matching the crime-scene ribbon dangling from its dilapidated porch. On Saturday night, two young men from the community delivered pizza to this vacant house, only to be waylaid. Law-enforcement officials say that they were shot execution-style...

Faulty Wiring Is Cited in Fire That Killed 4
Fire officials say that fire that killed four men in Maspeth, Queens, on April 20 was caused by faulty wiring and that owner, Frank Glowacki, had apparently tinkered with wiring so that he could siphon electricity to a shed in back of house; Mayor Rudolph Giuliani and several City Council members note that fire occurred in house that had been illegally subdivided; renew calls for crackdown on such illegal conversions; photo; graph (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Bias Suits Increase Over English-Only Rules
Increasing number of bias lawsuits are growing out of employers' strict English-only policies; one is an Equal Employment Opportunity Commission lawsuit that accuses Long Life Home Care Service of dismissing Maria Quinones and Evelyn Silverman for refusing request by their supervisors not to speak Spanish in casual conversations at the job; EEOC alleges violation of law that prohibits discrimination on basis of nationality; photo (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
From Pages Of the Torah, A Passion
David Gonzalez column interviews Rebbetzin Esther Jungreis on the importance for Jews of studying the Torah; her passionate lectures on the Torah at Manhattan synagogue draws hundreds of young professionals (M)

Much Ignorance of First Aid
Study conducted by University of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey and Eagleton Institute finds that most New Jersey residents are not equipped to deal with medical emergencies (New Jersey Daily Briefing) (S)

Auto Insurance Plan Revised
On the eve of a rally planned by drivers demanding lower insurance rates, Gov. Christine Todd Whitman announced yesterday that she would temporarily abandon her proposal to offer less coverage for lower rates and move ahead with other parts of her plan certain to win quick approval in the Legislature.

Titles10ON THE UNABOMBER TRACK: THE OVERVIEW;Ex-Professor Is Seized In Montana as Suspect In the Unabom AttacksCRASH IN THE BALKANS: THE OVERVIEW;COMMERCE SECRETARY AMONG 33 LOST IN A CROATIA PLANE CRASHCRASH IN THE BALKANS: IN WASHINGTON;Jet Crash Casts a Sudden Shadow Over Official WashingtonCRASH IN THE BALKANS: THE RESCUE;A Storm-Swept Mountain's Grim StoryRecalling Dodgers' Flight, Mayor Makes His Pitch for New StadiumNEW JERSEY DAILY BRIEFING;Lobbying Pays Off in a RaiseNEW JERSEY DAILY BRIEFING;Criticizing School FinancingNEW JERSEY DAILY BRIEFING;Police Shooting JustifiableMetro Matters;Slowly Moving Down a Road To a RoadwayNEW JERSEY DAILY BRIEFING;Turnpike Saves $6 Million
ON THE UNABOMBER TRACK: THE OVERVIEW;Ex-Professor Is Seized In Montana as Suspect In the Unabom Attacks
Federal agents today raided a remote Montana cabin where they seized a onetime university professor suspected of being the Unabomber, the elusive terrorist who has left a 17-year nationwide trail of mail bombs that have killed 3 people and maimed 23 others. Law-enforcement officials said tonight that the agents had found explosive chemicals and other bomb-making material at the wilderness cabin belonging to the suspect, Theodore J. Kaczynski. They said they planned to charge Mr. Kaczynski on Thursday with the series of deadly attacks, which had long baffled the authorities.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
CRASH IN THE BALKANS: THE OVERVIEW;COMMERCE SECRETARY AMONG 33 LOST IN A CROATIA PLANE CRASH
A military plane carrying Commerce Secretary Ronald H. Brown and a delegation of American corporate executives slammed into a mountainside today as it approached the airport at Dubrovnik, on the Adriatic coast of Croatia. Chances that Mr. Brown had survived were "next to zero," a White House official said after more than 12 hours of rescue efforts. Clambering over rocky, rugged terrain, working by flashlight in pelting rain, Croatian search parties found nine bodies and one survivor, described by a Dubrovnik doctor at the scene as "a woman who was bleeding profusely." She died later, Croatian officials said.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
CRASH IN THE BALKANS: IN WASHINGTON;Jet Crash Casts a Sudden Shadow Over Official Washington
At 10:30 this morning, President Clinton's national security adviser, Anthony Lake, walked into the Oval Office with the news: Commerce Secretary Ronald H. Brown's plane was missing in the Balkans and wreckage had been spotted. The rest was a welter of unconfirmed reports. In that moment, a brilliant spring day turned to ashes. Minutes later, the deputy White House press secretaries, Ginny Terzano and Mary Ellen Glynn, crept into the office of their boss, Michael D. McCurry, with news service bulletins and spirited him off to the top-secret Situation Room in the basement of the West Wing.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
CRASH IN THE BALKANS: THE RESCUE;A Storm-Swept Mountain's Grim Story
Croatian special police officers wrapped in green army ponchos, their faces averted from withering sheets of rain, labored up the steep slope of St. John Mountain tonight in a huge search for the far-flung wreckage that had been the plane carrying Secretary of Commerce Ronald H. Brown and 32 others.

Recalling Dodgers' Flight, Mayor Makes His Pitch for New Stadium
Recalling how New York City lost the Dodgers after a previous mayor refused to help the team build a new baseball stadium in Brooklyn, Mayor Rudolph W. Giuliani has begun to offer the economic justification -- and with it the political one -- for building the Yankees a new home on the West Side of Manhattan. In news conferences on Tuesday and again yesterday, Mr. Giuliani said that while he felt an emotional bond with Yankee Stadium dating to his boyhood memories of Yankee heroes, the city had to do all it could to insure that the team's owner, George...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
NEW JERSEY DAILY BRIEFING;Lobbying Pays Off in a Raise
Lobbyists in New Jersey are spending more, but not for the wining and dining of legislators, the state's Election Law Enforcement Commission said yesterday. Last year, lobbyists reported spending $76,083 on government officials and their staffs, or one-half of 1 percent of their total spending. In 1993 and 1994, they spent 1 percent, the commission said. Frederick M. Herrmann, the commission's executive director, could not explain the decline. But other spending by lobbyists did increase last year. One of the largest single expenditures by lobbying firms was lobbyists' salaries, which increased from $9 million in 1994 to $10 million last...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
NEW JERSEY DAILY BRIEFING;Criticizing School Financing
A majority of New Jersey voters agree with Governor Whitman and the State Supreme Court that financing disparities for rich and poor school districts should be eliminated, according to a poll released yesterday. The poll, conducted independently by the Quinnipiac College Polling Institute, also found that 78 percent of the 908 registered voters questioned believed the state, not local school districts, should set education standards. A narrow majority of voters said they would be willing to pay higher taxes to improve education in poorer districts. TERRY PRISTIN
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
NEW JERSEY DAILY BRIEFING;Police Shooting Justifiable
A Middlesex County grand jury has concluded that two New Jersey state troopers used justifiable force in December when they fatally shot a 19-year-old West Virginia man, The Associated Press reported yesterday. The troopers, Brian Largey and Joseph Caliguari, said they believed the man, David Gibson, was reaching for a gun when they shot him to death near a New Jersey Turnpike rest area after pursuing him and another youth for speeding in a stolen car. Mr. Gibson's family has maintained that he was unarmed, and no weapon was recovered.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Metro Matters;Slowly Moving Down a Road To a Roadway
THAT flamboyant display of euphoria the other day, when politicians finally broke ground for a renovated roadway on the western edge of Manhattan, is understandable. New York is a place that is letting one of its most valuable sites (Columbus Circle) go to ruin, that cannot keep its schools or subway stations from crumbling, and that gets hauled into court on almost every move it makes. So there is reason to celebrate when something actually stands a chance of being accomplished in New York. However, anyone tempted to join the cheers for what is variously called Route 9A, Westway Too...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
NEW JERSEY DAILY BRIEFING;Turnpike Saves $6 Million
Using part-time toll collectors on weekends saved the New Jersey Turnpike Authority $6 million in overtime costs in 1995, the agency said yesterday. Overtime was reduced by 37.5 percent because of work-rule changes put into effect last year, said Lynn Fleeger, a spokeswoman for the Turnpike Authority. Ms. Fleeger said additional overtime savings would be realized in 1996 by using temporary workers to fill in for vacationing, pregnant or injured full-time workers.

Moving Bits, Bytes and Books To the Library of the Future;A New Branch Offers Data in Old Forms and New
It was, perhaps, the ultimate illustration of style over substance. Just beyond the glitter of the Seventh on Sixth fashion shows in Bryant Park last week, a more mundane operation was trudging ahead, but one likely to affect New York for far longer. As designers and their fast-aging models displayed the pricey wares that will be out of date by next fall, movers were trucking 1.2 million books dealing with business, economics, chemistry, physics and mathematics, plus microfilm and microfiche -- 31 miles of material, if you stacked it up side by side -- from the central research edifice of...

ON THE SUSPECT'S TRAIL: THE INVESTIGATION;Long and Twisting Trail Led To Unabom Suspect's Arrest
The tip came the way the F.B.I. had long expected, from a family member with misgivings. But the search for the man investigators believe is the Unabomber still had months to go before the suspect was arrested on Wednesday, as his deeply torn family struggled with its loyalties and then as an elite team of agents stood vigil for weeks in the Montana snows. And even now the case is far from finished, postal inspectors and other law-enforcement officials said today. With the arrest of Theodore J. Kaczynski in Montana, hundreds of agents, held back in recent weeks for fear...

CRASH IN THE BALKANS: THE ISSUES;Plane Crash in Croatia Silenced A Big Player in Capital Debates
When Ronald H. Brown died on a hillside near Dubrovnik, Croatia, on Wednesday, he was in the thick of three battles here that touched the divergent roles he played in Washington. The first was a continuing struggle within the Administration -- sometimes still heated three years after it began -- over the degree to which the nation's commercial interests should drive its foreign policy agenda.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
CRASH IN THE BALKANS: THE OVERVIEW;Bad Equipment Tied to Crash, Perry Suggests
Defense Secretary William J. Perry suggested today that malfunctioning instruments may have caused the crash of an Air Force jet that took the life of Commerce Secretary Ronald H. Brown and at least 32 others in Croatia on Wednesday. Returning from a trip to Egypt, Mr. Perry told reporters on his plane, "It was a classic sort of accident that good instrumentation should be able to prevent."
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
ON THE SUSPECT'S TRAIL: THE OVERVIEW;Suspect Arraigned on One Bomb Count
Theodore J. Kaczynski, the onetime university professor taken into custody on Wednesday as a suspect in the Unabom case, was arraigned here today on a single felony charge of possessing bomb components and was held without bail. The arraignment followed a search in which, Federal authorities said, they found evidence that Mr. Kaczynski had turned his one-room mountain cabin, 50 miles to the northwest near the town of Lincoln, into a virtual bomb laboratory.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Yet Another Shift in Mayor's Circle: Deputy Mayor for Finance Leaves
Further consolidating his inner circle of trusted aides in top jobs, Mayor Rudolph W. Giuliani announced yesterday that John S. Dyson would step down as Deputy Mayor for finance and economic development and would be replaced by Fran Reiter, a political confidante. The changes follow by one week Mr. Giuliani's decision to place another loyal ally, Howard Safir, in the Police Commissioner's job, which was part of a flurry of other top-level shifts in the upper ranks of the administration.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
New York Official Lobbied for Agency Seeking City Business
Last April, when a Queens social-services organization was trying to win a multimillion-dollar contract with the city, it sent an unusual salesman to the city agency that was evaluating competing bids. The salesman, it turned out, was a city official himself. He is Albert J. Farina, a veteran official with the Department of Employment, and he had a stake in the outcome, too. The Queens agency had offered him a job, and six months later, after the city gave the agency two contracts worth a total of $43 million, Mr. Farina took a $75,000-a-year position with it.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
New Jersey Daily Briefing;Gains Cited in Camden Patrol
State troopers assigned to patrol dangerous neighborhoods in Camden said yesterday that their three-month effort was a success, The Associated Press reported. The police have made 1,069 arrests and have confiscated 86 weapons and 74 stolen cars, said Col. Carl A. Williams, the state police superintendent. Last year, 60 people were killed in Camden. So far this year, the city has recorded five homicides.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
New Jersey Daily Briefing;Vote on School Unit's Future
A newly formed coalition of parents and other residents in Union County is advocating a "yes" vote to dissolve the state's first regional high school district when the issue goes before voters on May 14. Parents in the district's wealthier towns -- Berkeley Heights, Kenilworth, Springfield and Mountainside -- said that since their property tax base determines their share of the cost, they are forced to pay $16,000 for each high school student. "I could send my kids to a private school and get a better education for cheaper," said Janet Glynos, president of Kenilworth's Committee of Concerned Parents. Officials...

Study Recommends The Yankees Move To a West Side Site
A consulting firm's recommendation that the Yankees move from the Bronx to the West Side of Manhattan was based on the conclusion that a new ball park along the Hudson River would produce a significant increase in attendance, higher prices for luxury seats and an enhanced image for New York City. The report, jointly commissioned by the city, the state and the Yankees, studied four options, including renovating the current Yankee Stadium, but came down solidly in favor of a West Side stadium with a retractable domed roof that could be used for baseball, football, concerts and conventions. The cost...

COUNCIL VOTES TO EASE SANCTIONS AGAINST BAGHDADYoung Doctors Find Specialist Jobs Hard to GetRwanda Jails: No Space, No Food, No JusticeWorld News Briefs; U.S. and North Korea Recess Nuclear TalksNo HeadlineSpanish Stirred by 'War' Over a Fish They Don't EatEmbargo Against Belgrade Has a Major LeakU.S. Looks Away as Iran Arms Bosnia
Pataki Proposes to Deny Shelter To Homeless Who Break Rules
The Pataki administration wants homeless people to be ejected from shelters if they fail to follow a variety of rules, including participating in drug treatment and job training programs, state officials said today. The State Social Services Department plans to issue regulations that would for the first time make the right to emergency shelter in New York State contingent on the willingness of recipients to comply with a strict code of conduct. The new rules would also require individuals and families in homeless shelters to search for permanent housing and to avoid drugs and violence.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Question in Bombing Trials: Can Two Masterminds Exist?
Ever since Ramzi Ahmed Yousef was arrested in Pakistan two months ago on charges that he engineered the World Trade Center explosion, Federal prosecutors have found themselves in a predicament in their efforts to prosecute Sheik Omar Abdel Rahman and 10 others in a related terrorism case. On the one hand, the Government is trying to prove that Mr. Abdel Rahman, a blind Egyptian cleric, was the spiritual leader of a band of terrorists who plotted to blow up not only the World Trade Center but also the United Nations and the Hudson River tunnels.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U.N. COUNCIL VOTES TO EASE SANCTIONS AGAINST BAGHDAD
The Security Council voted unanimously today to offer Iraq easier terms under which to sell oil to meet its urgent need for medicine, food and other essentials. If Iraq accepts the plan, it would be the first break in the wall of sanctions that has surrounded the country and constricted its economy since Iraq invaded Kuwait in 1990.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Young Doctors Find Specialist Jobs Hard to Get
Five years ago, Dr. Jonathan Boniuk would have been guaranteed overnight success. An eye surgeon trained at top medical schools, he would have walked into a busy practice and earned over $150,000 a year. But in the last month he has talked to friends, colleagues, friends of friends, recruiters, health maintenance organizations and drug company representatives who make the rounds of doctors' offices, all with the dwindling hope of finding a permanent job. He has had little success.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Rwanda Jails: No Space, No Food, No Justice
The prisoners cannot sit or lie down because there is no room. So they stand, four to a square yard, in the muddy central courtyard. It takes hours to reach the latrines, or the cooking fires where they eat. There is no protection against the daily downpours, and many of the prisoners' bare feet are rotting from gangrene. Every day 5 to 10 die and many more are taken to a local hospital with pneumonia and dysentery.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
World News Briefs; U.S. and North Korea Recess Nuclear Talks
Negotiators for the United States and North Korea have returned to their capitals to prepare for what could be a final round of talks next week on a deal aimed at preventing North Korea from developing nuclear weapons. The two sides met on Wednesday and Thursday in Berlin. Although talks had been expected to resume today, a terse announcement said they would recess until Tuesday.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
No Headline
"It sure would have been helpful in May of 1967, when I volunteered for Vietnam, if he had said then that the war was unwinnable." -- MAX CLELAND, former V.A. director, who lost both legs in Vietnam, referring to Robert S. McNamara. [ 7:1. ]

Spanish Stirred by 'War' Over a Fish They Don't Eat
In the Corral de la Moreria, a tiny nightclub specializing in flamenco, the lights were dim, the drinks half drunk and the walls still reverberating from the pounding of heels and the staccato clatter of castanets. It was time for some light repartee. A dancer sauntered across the stage and asked another, "Say, can you tell me how to get to Newfoundland?"

Embargo Against Belgrade Has a Major Leak
In spite of appeals from the United States, a senior official in the Italian Foreign Ministry says Rome can do little to stop Italian oil companies from selling fuel to Albania, though most of the fuel is apparently being smuggled to Serbia in violation of a United Nations embargo. The official said it was up to Albania to control the diversion.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U.S. Looks Away as Iran Arms Bosnia
The Clinton Administration has chosen to ignore weapons shipments from Iran to the Muslim-led Bosnian Government that in the last year have added considerably to Bosnia's military firepower, American officials said today. They said the United States has ample evidence of the Iranian deliveries, which are in violation of the United Nations embargo that bans arms shipments to all combatants in the Bosnian civil war. But they said the Administration had not tried in any way to cut off the flow. The Administration said last fall that it would no longer take part in efforts to enforce the embargo on...

OIL PROPOSALThe Sands Give Up an Ancient ShipThe View From: Middletown; Burglars Beware. Fingerprints Are Going Computerized.
CLINTON LAYS OUT THREE PRIORITIES FOR LEGISLATION
President Clinton today laid out a short list of his priorities in his next round of wrangling with the Republican Congress, saying that he was intent on winning passage of tax cuts linked to education, a welfare overhaul that promotes work and anti-crime measures that preserve the ban on assault-style weapons. The three-point list, which Mr. Clinton presented in his weekly radio address, offered the sharpest vision yet of his agenda for the months ahead and represented his most forceful attempt to win back control of the capital's agenda.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
For Elderly Immigrants, a Retirement Plan in U.S.
It was in the chaotic din of a Chinatown garment factory that 74-year-old Ho Yin-peng discovered a better way to survive than snipping loose threads from clothing at 5 cents a dress. Her co-workers told her that even though she lived with her son, she could qualify for the Federal welfare program known as Supplemental Security Income if she was over 65 and had been in this country for more than three years.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
In the Forefront of the Fight Against Disease
AMONG flasks, beakers, test tubes, and machines for molecular cloning, DNA cycling and polymerase-chain reactions, Kaie Ojamaa huddles over a high-powered microscope, peering at a mosaic pattern to detect rats' pulsating cardiac cells. She and her colleagues have added human thyroid hormone in experiments resulting in what they suspect may be significant advances for patients who have had open-heart surgery.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Tactics for the Disabled: Niceness Has Limits
DAVID KLOT burst out of the basement at 8:10 and smacked into the morning, a thin smile beneath his rakish moustache. Zipped into black leather, his tweed cap pulled over his dark glasses, he looked faintly dangerous. He gunned his Triumph up East 15th Street while I trotted along on the sidewalk. He can jack that baby up to 5.4 miles an hour. A bus pulled up on Park Avenue South as we arrived. Mr. Klot wheeled the scooter around while the driver lowered the lift. Mr. Klot needed half a dozen tries before he was able to back in...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U.S. Is Critical Of Tokyo Plan To Rein In Yen
Treasury Secretary Robert E. Rubin told his Japanese counterpart this morning that the emergency financial package Japan issued last week was so vague that it had failed to reverse the fall of the dollar against the yen. And the Secretary pressed for far more action before the currency gyrations threaten the economic stability of the world's biggest trading region. The meeting with Japan's Finance Minister, Masayoshi Takemura, came at the opening of a meeting of finance ministers from around the Pacific on the resort island of Bali. A senior Administration official who took part in the session said that Mr...

Courthouse Set for Islip Is Target Of Senate
THE Senate has cut $27.2 million from the financing for a new Federal courthouse in Suffolk County, raising questions about whether the project, whose final cost would exceed $200 million, can survive when Congress is slashing many programs. The cut, part of $1.8 billion in reduced spending for Federal construction projects, was approved, 79 to 15, this month. Senator Alfonse M. D'Amato, formerly the most influential supporter of the courthouse, voted for the reduction, causing confusion and apprehension among some Long Island officials.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
A Job Opening in Brooklyn: Poet Laureate
The Brooklyn poet laureate's study is unused now. There are an old memo pad, books and photographs in the tiny, lamplighted Brooklyn Heights den. A walking stick, polished but untouched, stands in a corner. And there is a poem taped to the bulletin board. It opens:

IRAQIS DENOUNCE U.N. OIL PROPOSAL
The Iraqi Government yesterday denounced a United Nations plan to allow limited sales of Iraqi oil and organized large anti-American demonstrations in Baghdad. "I think the voices of the masses have expressed the Iraqi stand," the speaker of the Iraqi Parliament, Saadi Mahdi Saleh, told Associated Press Television during one rally. "What you heard from the people is rejection of this decision."

The Sands Give Up an Ancient Ship
NOT too many years after the American Revolution and long before the Americans and British had settled into a generally peaceful, neutral relationship, privateering on the high seas was prevalent. And the Royal Navy was determined to put a stop to the fast ships that slipped out of the mouth of the Connecticut River at Essex and Old Lyme. Although historical accounts of the burning of Essex give readers a picture of what probably happened in the War of 1812, new physical evidence, in the form of the skeleton of a sleek vessel, perhaps what is known as a sharp...

The View From: Middletown; Burglars Beware. Fingerprints Are Going Computerized.
ON television crime shows, the most difficult case is often cracked by a criminal's careless slip in leaving a single fingerprint at the scene. But in real life, as any detective will tell you, that's a very unlikely scenario. The reason is that a search through a million or more fingerprint cards in a state's central fingerprint depository is an all but impossible task unless investigators have a known suspect whose prints are already on file.

Women Make Gains but Meet Obstacles In Construction and Contracting Trades
WHEN Linda Petrizzo renovated an old home she bought several years ago, she had no notions about starting a new career. She was just desperate to work her way out of a jam when a contractor she had hired backed out. But today, as a general contractor and co-owner of Women on the Job in Keyport, Ms. Petrizzo remodels homes, paints, sands, spackles and replaces plumbing for a living. The new career that grew out of her own home improvement work makes her happier than any job she has ever had, she says, but it also makes her rare: She...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Malls and the Like Mean Business In the Shoreline Towns Along I-95
INTERSTATE 95 between Guilford and Old Saybrook is flanked by dense woods, rocky outcroppings and small ponds. Occasionally, the natural setting thins to reveal houses or small businesses. But lately, signs of development are appearing, as shoreline towns look for ways to give their economies a shot in the arm. The most ambitious project, a 400,000-square-foot mall off Exit 65 in Westbrook, is expected to be under construction late this spring. A similar, smaller mall may follow in Clinton. Both are expected to attract outlet stores like Polo Ralph Lauren, Liz Claiborne, Reebok, Magnavox and Vanity Fair.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Father Pours His Grief and His Life Into an Unusual Toy Line
A FATHER'S desire to turn his grief over the death of a prematurely born son into messages of inspiration to other children has fashioned a new group of nonviolent toys. In his New Brunswick office, the father, Stephen Bacque, has created a world of stories and toys dedicated to Paul Joseph Bacque, who was born prematurely in 1991. The baby lived for only three and a half hours but became the force behind a thriving, award-winning business. The toys, called the P. J.'s line, take a stand against guns and drugs while stimulating a child's imagination and motor skills. They...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Construction Equipment Damage May Have Caused Gas Explosion
Federal investigators found evidence today that a rupture in a gas pipeline that touched off a powerful explosion at an apartment complex here may have been caused by repeated damage to the pipeline from construction equipment. After searching a jagged crater carved by the blast near Durham Woods apartments, the investigators said they found deep scratches on nearly every piece of the splintered pipeline they picked up. They said the marks may have been made by digging equipment belonging to an asphalt plant, owned by Quality Materials, that is so near the pipeline that its fuel tank was wrecked in...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Nature and Economic Boom Devouring China's Farmland
China's frenetic economic boom and natural forces are shrinking the country's farmland at an alarming rate, scientists and Government officials say. As a result, Chinese and Western scientists are raising new questions about the country's ability to feed itself in the future. In China's central and eastern provinces, the country's breadbasket for three millenniums, farmers are abandoning the land to chase prosperity in big cities and towns. There, tens of thousands of new factories have opened under the economic reforms of Deng Xiaoping, China's paramount leader.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
In North Bosnia, a Rising Tide of Serbian Violence
While the world's attention has been focused on the cease-fire in Sarajevo and diplomatic moves to broker peace elsewhere in the shattered former Yugoslavia, a savage Serbian campaign to drive Muslims and Croats from land seized in the last two years is continuing, even growing, in the Banja Luka region of northern Bosnia. "It's business as usual in Banja Luka; it's even getting worse and worse," said Joren Bjallerstedt, the chief protection officer for the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees in Zagreb, the capital of Croatia. "Ethnic cleansing, violence toward minorities has increased in the last month, clearly."
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Cold Shoulder to Churches That Practice Preachings
Never mind the biblical injunctions to feed the poor and shelter the homeless. Increasingly, many Americans do not want the social service programs of religious institutions in their neighborhoods. They frequently don't want the parking problems or pealing bells that come with churches. They don't want programs for alcoholics or drug addicts. They especially don't want the hungry and the homeless that these institutions often feed and shelter.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
WHITE HOUSE AIDE BECOMES SUBJECT OF NEW INQUIRIES
The White House and the Whitewater special prosecutor are conducting separate inquiries into whether a senior aide to President Clinton acted improperly last month by raising questions about the hiring of a high-profile Republican lawyer to look into the bankruptcy of an Arkansas savings and loan. Thomas F. McLarty 3d, the White House chief of staff, ordered the internal review on Friday night after learning that the prosecutor was looking into a conversation on Feb. 25 between George Stephanopoulos, one of Mr. Clinton's closest aides, and Joshua Steiner, a close friend of Mr. Stephanopoulos who is the Treasury Department chief...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Relocated Office Jobs May Signal a New Weakness
THE senior planner for Long Island, Lee E. Koppelman, warned last week that a decision by the National Westminster Bank to move nearly 1,000 jobs to Scranton, Pa., poses grave implications for the regional economy because other service businesses trying to cut costs might also depart. "The loss of Natwest may be more serious and more dangerous to the Long Island economy than the loss of Grumman," said Mr. Koppelman, executive director of the Long Island Regional Planning Board and the chief planner for the region since the 60's.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Fish Market's Problems Revert to New York City
Eight years ago the top Federal prosecutor in Manhattan began a crackdown against the Mafia that led to extensive Federal oversight of the Fulton Fish Market, the nation's largest wholesale fish and seafood distribution center. Now the headaches of running the market and combating mobsters have come full circle to the former prosecutor, Rudolph W. Giuliani, New York's new Mayor. As Federal intervention wanes, it is Mr. Giuliani's administration that will be faced with directly supervising the market as the city resumes responsibility for it for the first time since 1982.

Wrestling Private Demons; Driver in Crash That Killed Priests Kept Problems to Herself
In the photograph her mother and father cling to now, Lisa Bongiorno is wearing a black, sleeveless, floor-length dress, holding a bouquet of flowers and half-turning with a little girl's impish smile. She is pretty, in a shy way, with dark hair and a slender neck, and she seems to be gliding away from the camera, off the edge of the picture. This was last November at her sister's wedding. There were little sparks of excitement dancing in the centers of Lisa Bongiorno's eyes. Now her shattered parents, eager to recall their daughter's joyfulness, cannot stare at the picture hard...

Patients' Lawyers Lead Insurers To Pay for Unproven Treatments
Pamela Schmale, a 39-year-old bookkeeper, says she felt that her only hope of surviving advanced breast cancer was to have a bone marrow transplant. But her insurance company said it would not pay for the expensive and risky procedure, which is still undergoing clinical testing. In desperation, Mrs. Schmale and her husband, Arthur, mortgaged their house in Boring, Ore., to raise the $100,000 or more they would need for the transplant. And her doctors recommended lawyers who might fight their insurance company for them. Cost of Legal Advice The Schmales hired Sheldon Weinhaus, a lawyer in St. Louis who persuaded...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
New York Widens Work Release To Reduce Prison Overcrowding
Work release, a program allowing some inmates a large measure of freedom before parole, is increasingly being used in New York State to relieve prison overcrowding, officials acknowledge. As a result, prisoners who would not have been eligible five years ago are enrolled, including some who have repeatedly been denied parole. As the state's Correction Commissioner makes room for newcomers in a prison system 29 percent over capacity, he says he is being forced to take too many chances with work-release participants. "We've expanded the work-release program to greater numbers than I feel comfortable with," said the Commissioner, Thomas A...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Whirlwind of a Manager Runs A Village's Daily Operations
Christopher Russo likes to say that he learned how to run a village government during an after-school job as a delivery boy at a produce market in Scarsdale. "They wanted the best quality," he said of his customers. "They wanted efficiency. They wanted the best price. Who does that sound like?"
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Britons Try All Contraptions To Halt Rampant Car Theft
When Sinclair Seymour bought a new Ford Mondeo station wagon two months ago, he took no chances. Not only did he make sure his car had a factory-equipped antitheft system, including deadbolts inside the doors, an alarm and a high-tech device that electronically locks the engine, but he also asked the dealer to install a backup alarm and mount a thick steel padlock over the gearshift. Then, for extra peace of mind, he spent $225 more for a yellow wheel clamp, similar to the heavy metal boots some American police departments use to immobilize parking scofflaws. Now, whenever Mr. Seymour...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Far North in Russia, the Mines' Fatal Blight
The snow falls black in this city 200 miles above the Arctic Circle, big flakes of freezing ash covering the ground, the rivers and what little is left alive. Once a place of unparalleled beauty, in just 30 years this stark region, the Kola peninsula, has become one of the most poisoned spots on earth -- relentlessly transformed by Russian industry into a laboratory of ecological destruction.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Tax Cut Plans Prompt Fight In Albany
After three months the debate over Gov. Mario M. Cuomo's proposed budget has finally swung full circle to where it was expected to conclude all along: an election-year scrap over how much to reduce taxes. Late last week, the Republicans who control the State Senate threw the budget talks into disarray by calling for tax cuts, mostly for businesses, totaling $700 million in the fiscal year that begins on Friday.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
DOZENS IN SOUTH DIE IN TORNADOES
The children of the congregation were in the middle of a Palm Sunday play this morning when a tornado ripped into their small church in northeastern Alabama, wrenching the roof apart and toppling a wall onto the children. At least 18 worshipers, among them six children, were killed, and almost 100 others were injured. "It is the most horrible thing I've ever seen," said Leon Smith, one of more than 100 rescue workers who responded to the calls for help after the tornado hit the Goshen Methodist Church at about 11:30 A.M.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
PULSE: Courts
According to the American Bar Association, civil cases should be settled or decided within 24 months, and felony cases should end withing one year. Around the country, these standards are not being met. Of the 181,526 civil cases pending in New Jersey last year, 15 percent were more than two years old.In Connecticut, almost half of the criminal cases pending last year were more than two years old. In Connecticut, almost half of the criminal cases pending last year were considered backlogged, and the median age of these cases was 6.8 months. Efforts are periodically made to chop away at...

Mexican Old Guard Fights Salinas on a New Candidate
As President Carlos Salinas de Gortari works to build consensus on choosing a replacement for the slain candidate who was to succeed him, he has come up against an unusually open challenge from members of the old guard of his governing party. After reports that Mr. Salinas might select Ernesto Zedillo, 42, the economist and former minister who was managing the campaign of Luis Donaldo Colosio, the governing-party candidate slain on Wednesday, party members began lobbying intensely on Saturday for favorites of their own.

When Day Care Turns Deadly; A Fire Kills 2 Boys and Stillness Now Unsettles Eleta Brown
Since the February afternoon when two little boys died in a fire in the basement of her house where she provided illegal day care, Eleta Brown, 82 years old, has stood at her window, lonely and bored, waiting wistfully for the day when authorities will allow her to tend children once more. Pampers are stuffed behind the candy-apple red vinyl couch in the living room. Stuffed animals have been put away in her bedroom dresser drawers. "I thought it would clear my mind, but it doesn't," she said.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Bosnians in Besieged Sarajevo Look Back on Year of Horror
Determined to find something for her four young children to eat, Stanka Voloder set out one day last week and walked five miles to the barbed-wire perimeter of the United Nations headquarters here to rummage through the garbage. After a while, she found three rotting potatoes and an onion, walked home to the Bistrik district in the city center, and cooked her treasures over a wood fire. As she told her story later, Mrs. Voloder broke down in tears. "Oh help me, please help me," she said over and over as she stood in Sarajevo's central market, clutching a bedroll...

In Unmasking Anonymity of Crime, Victims Face Inmates
As Ralph H. Hubbard spoke of another commonplace murder in the city, the sidewalk slaying of his son, his face was a weathered, Jobian mask of tolerance that made his jailhouse visit all the more fascinating to the assembled convicts who listened and wondered about his feelings. "Tragic as it is, I will never see the logic of it," Mr. Hubbard said, recalling the "silly" circumstance -- a dispute over a videotape -- that led the killer later to ambush his 23-year-old son, Brett, in a hail of gunfire. "I will never see the rationale. I will never see my...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
SENATE SUSPENDS EFFORT FOR ACCORD ON CLINTON'S PLAN
Nine days into a filibuster and miles from any hint of agreement, Senate Democrats and Republicans abandoned the hunt for a compromise on President Clinton's economic stimulus package tonight, and decided to try again in two weeks. The Democratic majority leader, Senator George J. Mitchell of Maine, said he decided to stop talking and send the Senate on a long-postponed vacation after Republicans refused to make an opening offer to resolve differences.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Poll Says Public Favors Changes In Health Policy
Fired by a sense of crisis, a majority of Americans say they are willing to accept substantial changes in their health-care system, including government price controls, new taxes and longer waits for nonemergency appointments, according to the latest New York Times/CBS News Poll. They have high expectations for President Clinton's promised health-care plan, the poll found, and they consider changes in health care an issue at least as urgent as the Federal deficit, which is near the top of the public's agenda.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U.S. Confirms F.B.I. Alerted By Egyptians
The State Department acknowledged today that the Egyptian authorities had warned the American authorities repeatedly in the months preceding the World Trade Center bombing about an Islamic fundamentalist network in the United States. But it sought to emphasize that Egypt had not relayed information about specific planned acts of terrorism.

Article 083593 -- No Title
After the most harmonious round of budget negotiations in four years, the State Legislature approved a $32.08 billion budget today that many lawmakers proclaimed as the start of a fiscal recovery for New York State. A late infusion of new money, generated at least partly by an economy that is growing faster than originally projected, allowed the Legislature and Gov. Mario M. Cuomo to provide an increase of $330 million to the state's schools over the last school year, to eliminate suggested cuts in Medicaid programs and to kill a number of proposed tax increases.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Budget Blur Leaves Some With Questions
After months of haggling, cajoling and deal-making, it all came to this: New York State's $32.08 billion budget was passed in a blur late Sunday and early Monday, so quickly that many legislators weren't quite sure what it said. "It's impossible to know what's in here," groused State Senator Franz S. Leichter, a Manhattan Democrat who was sagging under the weight of the 500-plus-page local assistance budget. He opened it up. "Here's $30,000 for fair radio communication on Long Island. What does that mean?" (The money will be used to promote radio stories about Long Island).

Italy's Scandal Spreads to 2 More Former Premiers
Accusations of wrongdoing against Italy's elder statesman, Giulio Andreotti, widened significantly today when magistrates in Milan formally notified him that he was under investigation in connection with the vast national bribery and corruption scandal. At the same time, the Italian news agency reported that another former Christian Democratic Prime Minister, Arnaldo Forlani, a high-ranking party official who headed a short-lived administration in 1981, was also under investigation. Whether to Lift Immunity The charges deepened the impression that Italy's postwar political system is rapidly unraveling. Even though the accusations today have yet to be judged in court, they mean that three...
