CONCERNS IN MOSCOW: MISSILES AND U.S. AND SOVIET LEADERS
The dominant impression from 10 days of conversations with officials in the Soviet Union is that they are waiting for developments in three areas: to see whether the United States will have the necessary political support to deploy new missiles in Western Europe; to gauge whether politics will push the Reagan Administration toward compromises, and, above all, to find out how the Soviet Union will ultimately fare under the new leadership of Yuri V. Andropov. Compared with past conversations with many of the same officials and academic experts, even after the Soviet intervention in Afghanistan in late 1979, there seemed...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
POWER PLANT ON S.I. DELAYED INDEFINITELY BY CUOMO MOVE
A plan to build a $2.1 billion coal- and garbage-fueled power plant on Staten Island on the shore of the Arthur Kill was ''indefinitely postponed'' yesterday by the Power Authority of the State of New York. The action came after Governor Cuomo informed the authority chairman, John S. Dyson, that he would not sign a contract for the sale of power from the proposed plant. Mr. Cuomo's decision, contained in a letter dated March 25, effectively blocked the authority from selling bonds, its sole means of raising money for large construction projects, according to Mr. Dyson. During his gubernatorial campaign...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
NO SIGN OF A HONDURAN MILITARY BUILDUP ON BORDER
A two-day trip through border areas in El Paraiso Province over the past weekend showed no indication that Honduras had reinforced its scattered outposts there. The border appeared as lightly patrolled as usual. Military sources here said there were only about 450 soldiers spread along the entire Honduran-Nicaraguan frontier, which stretches nearly 500 miles from coast to coast. Army units in the area did not appear to have any sophisticated monitoring capacity. Diplomats in the capital said the Honduran military did not have the equipment or trained personnel to compile and maintain accurate information about what was happening in all...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
CONVENTION HALL MAY FACE DELAYS OF YEAR OR MORE
Completion of the New York City Convention Center will be delayed by at least a year and the cost will be significantly higher than previously projected, according to a report by a vice president of the state's Urban Development Corporation. ''I am in possession of a report that shows the possibility of significant increases in costs and significant slippage in completion,'' said William Stern, the new chairman and president of the state corporation. George Schoepfer, president and chief executive of the Convention Center Development Corporation, a state agency, said: ''At this stage of the project we can't confirm that we're...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U.S. WOULD ASK CHILDREN TO PAY FOR PATIENT CARE
Federal officials, in a new interpretation of the Medicaid law, say that states may require people to help pay for care provided to their parents in nursing homes. The ruling would give states a new way to curtail Medicaid costs by requiring people to share some of the financial burden now borne by government. But Federal officials acknowledged that there might be legal and practical problems in enforcing such a concept of ''family responsibility,'' and a House subcommittee chairman said the ruling was contrary to the intent of Congress. Middle-Income Families Medicaid, whose costs are shared by Federal and state...

Transcript of interview, page A14.
President Reagan said today that if the United States was able to develop a comprehensive defense against missiles, he could foresee some president offering to share it with the Soviet Union to encourage the elimination of all offensive nuclear missiles. Arguing that critics of his call last week for research into missile defense were ''irresponsible,'' Mr. Reagan said it was ''unthinkable'' that the only way that peace is maintained between the United States and the Soviet Union is because ''no one pulls the trigger'' and launches ''these horrible missiles aimed at each other.'' Mr. Reagan was interviewed by six reporters...

COLLEGE UNITS ASK CURB ON HECKLING
Alarmed by the disruption of speakers on the nation's campuses, organizations representing college presidents, faculty members and students urged the academic community yesterday to ''respect the right of others to listen to those who have been invited to speak on campus.'' The unusual joint plea was prompted in particular by campus incidents involving Jeane J. Kirkpatrick, the chief United States delegate to the United Nations, who in recent months has been prevented from speaking several times or has been compelled to do so under difficult conditions. Academic officials say they have been worried about their ability to maintain an open...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
INSURERS BALKING ON COMPANY LOSSES
American companies and their insurers are embroiled in bitter legal clashes over who will bear the costs of major corporate disasters. Many corporations facing unexpected liabilities have found their insurers unwilling to pay their claims. Insurance payments have been refused, at least in part, in cases ranging from the asbestos claims against the Manville Corporation to the costs of the recall that followed the deaths of seven people from cyanide-laced Tylenol capsules. More and more, corporations are taking their insurers to court to try to force them to pay. The disputes over coverage are now so severe that one underwriter...

SECRETARY OF STATE WILL SOUND OUT BOTH SIDES ON AVERTING HOSTILITIES
President Reagan decided today to send Secretary of State Alexander M. Haig Jr. to London and Buenos Aires to sound out those two Governments on ways of avoiding a military clash over the Falkland Islands. The decision was made early this morning before Mr. Reagan left for Jamaica and the start of his vacation in the Caribbean. Mr. Haig had been scheduled to accompany Mr. Reagan, but instead Deputy Secretary Walter J. Stoessel Jr. will represent the State Department. The United States has avoided taking sides in the Falklands sovreignty dispute, hoping to create an atmosphere favorable to diplomatic efforts...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
METROPOLITAN REGION DIGGING OUT AFTER BLIZZARD LEADS TO 8 DEATHS
To the scrape of snow shovels on slushy walks and the whine of reluctant tires on snowbound streets, the metropolitan area began to recover yesterday from the first recorded April blizzard. Bitter winds buffeted pedestrians, traffic was slow and occasionally perilous, buses and trains reported delays and the airports were crowded with travelers trying to get out of the snow belt for the Passover and Easter holidays. At least eight men, most of them over the age of 60, died in the New York metropolitan area from the exertion of shoveling snow. Although the National Weather Service said the worst...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
CONGRESS IS IN DOUBT OVER COST AND NEED IN AIR FORCE BUILDUP
After nearly a decade in which the Air Force insisted that it was falling behind the Soviet Union, the Reagan Administration has ordered the service to embark on a major buildup in manpower, fighter planes, bombers and strategic weapons. Critics on Capitol Hill argue that the Administration's proposals are unselective, unnecessary and uncreative. The critics acknowledge that the bulk of the Air Force programs will probably be approved this year, but they say the debate will intensify in the years ahead. The service's proposed growth in the fiscal year 1983 over last year, beyond that needed to account for inflation...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
PHILADELPHIA DOCTOR SUSPECTED AS THIEF OF TASTE AND DARING
To some of his acquaintances here, Dr. Frank Waxman was a caring and successful young doctor. Others also knew him as an art collector of considerable taste and knowledge. The 37-year-old Philadelphian, a graduate of the University of Pennsylvania and the Philadelphia College of Osteopathic Medicine, lived quietly with his 35-year-old wife, Julie, in a luxury apartment house overlooking historic Rittenhouse Square. Occasionally he would entertain guests from the art world in a 13th-floor apartment adorned with paintings and sculptures, all as softly and skillfully lighted as if they were in a museum.

HEFNER REFUSED CASINO LICENSE IN ATLANTIC CITY
New Jersey's Casino Control Commission ruled today that Playboy Enterprises was unsuitable to operate a casino in Atlantic City unless it severed ties with its founder and principal owner, Hugh M. Hefner. The commission gave Playboy until Tuesday to make its decision. Frank P. DiPrima, senior vice president and general counsel of the company, said he would appeal the commission's decision in court and seek a stay of any action to bar Playboy from Atlantic City until the appeal was decided. The commission's 3-to-2 vote to license Mr. Hefner and the company was one short of the four needed for...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
EX-AIDES LINK REP. RICHMOND TO DRUG BUYING
Former aides of Representative Frederick W. Richmond have told a Federal grand jury that the Brooklyn Congressman periodically paid them and others to buy him cocaine and marijuana, according to sources familiar with the accounts. The accounts, the sources said, have named as participants in the illegal drug transactions at least nine present and former aides, several of whom have testified before the grand jury investigating the four-term Democrat. One former senior staff member, Barry Nickelsberg, has asserted that, at Mr. Richmond's request, he purchased gram quantities of cocaine in New York City on four occasions in 1980. Afterward, he...

MEN OF FAITH REFLECT ON ANSWERING A CALL
The call comes differently to each man, a message surrounded by mystery. For Vincent Keane, it was a quiet voice that he finally decided to answer. For Michael Calvin, it was a persistent summons he could no longer resist. Neither can say why the herald came to them and not to others. It is, they are certain, a divine enigma, one of the Creator's bewildering gifts. With the approach of Easter and the Feast of the Resurrection, Mr. Keane and Mr. Calvin, Roman Catholics and candidates for the priesthood at the Immaculate Conception Seminary here, were asked to share their...

Excerpts from article, page A14.
Four former senior Government officials urged the United States and its European allies today to consider a pledge not to be the first to use nuclear weapons in Europe. To compensate, they recommended the strengthening of the West's conventional forces on the continent. In a magazine article and a joint statement, the four contended that the buildup of nuclear arsenals, divisions in the Atlantic alliance and official contingency planning for fighting limited nuclear war now make it imperative for the Western powers to abandon their three-decade-old strategy of threatening to counter a conventional Soviet attack in Europe with nuclear weapons...

HAIG PLANS MEDIATION TRIPS
Britain threatened tonight to sink any Argentine ship that came within 200 miles of the Falkland Islands after Monday morning. John Nott, the Defense Secretary, made the announcement at the end of a seven-hour debate in the House of Commons on the seizure of the South Atlantic archipelago by Argentine troops Friday. The disclosure, which amounted to the imposition of a blockade, curtailed the time available for efforts to find a peaceful solution. ADMINISTRATION SEEKS EASED RULES FOR INDUSTRIES IN CLEAN WATER ACT
The Reagan Administration is proposing 14 changes in the Clean Water Act, among them deletion of the rule that mandatory national standards must be established for treatment of industrial toxic wastes. Legislation drafted by the Environmental Protection Agency, which was circulated today by environmental groups, also proposes a fouryear delay in the 1984 deadline for industry to install the ''best available technology'' to treat such wastes. However, the draft legislation would not permit individual companies to obtain waivers of the ''best technology' ' rule, a change urgently sought by some sectors of industry. Nor would the draft lessen the law's...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
KEAN SAID TO PICK BUSINESSMAN FOR SENATE SEAT HELD BY WILLIAMS
Governor Kean has chosen Nicholas F. Brady, a business executive, to fill the United States Senate seat vacated by Harrison A. Williams Jr., leaders of the New Jersey Republican Party said today. Carl Golden, Mr. Kean's press secretary, said the Governor planned to announce his decision early next week. Efforts to reach Mr. Kean and Mr. Brady for confirmation were unsuccessful. Neither the Governor nor Mr. Brady would return repeated phone calls to their homes and offices.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
RICHMOND IS ASKED BY ESPOSITO TO QUIT FOR THE PARTY'S SAKE
The Brooklyn Democratic leader, Meade H. Esposito, yesterday called on Representative Frederick W. Richmond, the subject of a Federal investigation, to ''resign immediately for the good of the Democratic Party.'' Mr. Esposito, a sponsor of Mr. Richmond's transition from a wealthy businessman to a holder of a public office, , said he was ''very upset'' about a Federal grand jury inquiry into various facets of the Brooklyn Democratic Congressman's activities. He said that he had asked Mr. Richmond to resign at a luncheon Wednesday and that Mr. Richmond had responded that he wanted time to think about it. Mr. Esposito...

HAIG AND BRITONS HOLD 6-HOUR TALKS ON THE FALKLANDS
Secretary of State Alexander M. Haig Jr. met with senior British officials tonight in his search for a peaceful way out of the Falklands crisis, but the Government of Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher continued to issue threats against Argentina. When the talks broke up, Mr. Haig said that his ''very long and very detailed discussions'' had left him ''highly impressed by the firm determination of the British Government.'' He and his aides spent more than six hours with Mrs. Thatcher and her senior associates, exploring the issues affecting the controversy. Defense Minister John Nott, who on Wednesday night announced the...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
M.T.A. OPEN TO PURCHASING MORE SUBWAY CARS ABROAD
The Metropolitan Transporation Authority is negotiating with two more foreign companies to buy subway cars following its purchase last month of 325 subway cars from a Japanese manufacturer. M.T.A. officials are talking to representatives of Bombardier Inc. of Canada and Francorail, a consortium of French companies, as well as to an American manufacturer, the Budd Company of Troy, Mich., about the purchase of 825 cars. The authority concluded a $274.5 million deal with Kawasaki Heavy Industries Ltd. of Japan for 325 cars on March 17. The United Steelworkers of America and the Specialty Steel Industry of the United States, in...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
DOLE SEES 'HORROR STORY' IN M.T.A. LEASING DEAL
Senator Bob Dole, the chairman of the Senate Finance Committee, today denounced as a ''horror story'' a $100 million leasing arrangement for New York buses and commuter rail cars. The arrangement, between the Metropolitan Transportation Authority and Metromedia, the broadcasting company, was worked out last December under the Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981. The act allows transit agencies to to raise money be transferring, through the sale of their equipment, depreciation benefits that they, as government agencies, cannot take advantage of. The M.T.A., which pressed for passage of the act, has said that it can save hundreds of millions...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
MALL STANDS ALONE IN BROOKLYN 'RENAISSANCE'
In October 1969, at the height of his campaign for re-election, Mayor John V. Lindsay unveiled a spectacular plan for the revitalization of downtown Brooklyn. When the campaign ended, most of the plan was forgotten. But one part survives, and some would say thrives. It is the Fulton Street Mall. The mall has not transformed downtown Brooklyn, nor has it brought the middle-class shopper back to Brooklyn, which some saw as its primary goal. Even so, it has injected new life into a dying business area, and if its proponents are right, it may mean a renaissance for what was...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
SOVERN WILL HEAD CITY CHARTER UNIT
Mayor Koch has selected Michael I. Sovern, the president of Columbia University, to head a new panel charged with recommending changes in New York City's Charter, the municipal equivalent of the Constitution. The primary responsibility of the panel, known as the Charter Revision Commission, will be to propose a way of electing at-large City Council members that does not violate the United States Constitution. Two recent Federal court rulings declared that the present way of electing two Council members from each borough and giving all 10 of them equal voting power violated the constitutional provison of one man, one vote...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
ARGENTINE ASSERTS WAR THREAT FADES
Argentina's Foreign Minister said today that ''the danger of war with Britain is fading.'' Speaking at the airport upon returning from Washington, the Minister, Nicanor Costa Mendez, said he was ''very optimistic'' about the chances of a peaceful settlement of the crisis with Britain over Argentina's seizure of the the Falkland Islands, a British colony. Mr. Costa Mendez said he based his optimism on the diplomatic effort begun today by Secretary of State Alexander M. Haig Jr., who flew to London to explore the possibilities of negotiations. The Secretary is due here after his London mission is completed.

ENVIRONMENTALISTS, SEEING THREAT IN WHITE HOUSE POLICY, PLAN FIGHT
Although it always was much more than a group of bird watchers in tweeds and sturdy shoes, the National Audubon Society has never been known as a hotbed of political activism. But times and the society are changing. Concerned over what they perceive as a serious threat to the environment posed by the policies and the appointments of the Reagan Administration, the Audubon Society and other old-line conservation groups such as the Wilderness Society and the National Wildlife Federation are breaking tradition and preparing for a major political effort in Washington and around the country. These organizations report that their...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
PRESIDENT, COMFORTABLE AT HOME, DELAYS HIS RETURN TO OVAL OFFICE
President Reagan has increased his workload to four or five hours a day and is essentially able to do the same amount of paperwork he did before he was shot in the chest March 30, White House officials say. But Mr. Reagan has only now begun to resume the one activity his aides said consumed most of his time before the assassination attempt: lobbying for his economic program. The President made his first telephone calls to Congressmen to push for the program yesterday afternoon. A week ago, when Mr. Reagan was released from George Washington University Hospital, his doctors said...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
PATERSON: STILL BEAUTIFUL RELIC TRYING HARD FOR COMEBACK
PATERSON FOR years, the soot-stained mills and factories around the Great Falls here churned out the engines of industrial expansion for a booming nation. Today, the bustling industrial district is a halfdeserted relic whose major distinction is historical. Some manufacturing is still going on, and one or two plants continue to use water power. But many of the buildings are empty, and the city is trying to turn the area into a business center and artist colony. It is to be a tourist attraction, the last thing in the world that would have occurred to the men who used the...

SAUDIS REBUFF U.S. ON POSTPONING DEAL FOR 5 RADAR PLANES
Saudi Arabia has rejected Reagan Administration suggestions that a political fight on Capitol Hill might be avoided if it postponed the purchase of five Awacs electronic surveillance planes and settled instead for equipment to increase the range and firepower of its previously ordered F-15 fighters, senior State Department officials said today. The officials, clearly concerned about the damage to future relations with the Saudis if, as expected, a major confrontation takes place on Capitol Hill over the Awacs, said that the Saudis appeared adamant on having the sale go ahead as part of one package, including the F-15 equipment and...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U.S. SAID TO REVISE STRATEGY TO OPPOSE THREATS BY SOVIET
The Reagan Administration has begun to fashion a military strategy that would prepare the armed forces for the possibility that some confrontation with the Soviet Union might lead to a protracted conventional war, according to senior Pentagon officials. The concept, which is basically set but is still being refined, reflects the initial imprint of Defense Secretary Caspar W. Weinberger and his top associates on the nation's military thinking. It is part of the policy being drafted to guide the services as they shape their 1983 budgets. The officials said the plan sheds the concept that any war with the Soviet...

SEWER WORK SPEEDS UP AMID ENVIRONMENTAL QUESTIONS
MINEOLA WHEN critics of the Southwest Sewer District charged five years ago that the fees for the project's design engineers were too high and that the scope of the sewer system was larger than necessary, its defenders cut short the criticisms by asserting that no government agency had ever found anything corrupt or improper about the project. Ten days ago, a Federal grand jury handed up indictments containing charges that, if proved, will show that from the outset the design engineer, contractors and politicians involved had every reason to make the project as costly as possible. ''What is so unfortunate,''...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Politics; DEBATE RISES OVER SEAGRAM AIRPORT SITE
TO residents of Rye, Port Chester and Greenwich, Conn., Westchester County's proposal to permit construction of a Seagram building at the County Airport has become a matter of development by stealth and is essentially unwelcome. To members of Westchester's Board of Legislators, who face a vote on approval of the hanger-office complex one week from tomorrow, it is ''government by crisis,'' as one of them put it, and a new confrontation with County Executive Alfred B. DelBello. And to Mr. DelBello, who says he has seen it happen before, it is ''politics, pure politics,'' with some Republican Legislators expressing doubts...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
REPUBLICANS SEEK BENEFIT FROM FIGHT OVER BUDGET
HARTFORD OUTSIDE the offices of the Republican leaders of the House of Representatives at the State Capitol is a chart that illustrates the political side of the General Assembly's scheduled debate this week on a new state budget. The chart, which looks like it should be part of a charity drive to record the upward march of contributions toward the goal, is labeled: ''Taxometer to Measure Democratic Spending Fever.'' By the end of last week, the red ink for new taxes and fees already passed by the legislature and the blue ink for other tax increases that have been proposed...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
BRONX RIVER FLOOD STUDY: NO U.S. AID WARRANTED
IN the last decade, torrential rains have swollen and flooded its streams on three occasions required Westchester County to be declared a Federal disaster area. Flood damage following a June 1972 storm amounted to more than $20 million and flood stages were two feet higher in September 1975, when Yonkers, in one example, experienced what was called the most damaging flood in its history. The Army Corps of Engineers has studied dozens of rivers and streams in the county since the mid-1950's and made 31 reports on flood-control investigations. Now it has concluded its research with a draft report on...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
FOR CUBAN REFUGEES, PROMISE OF U.S. FADES
A year ago, clinging to the rails of small boats tossing in the waterspouts and squalls of the Florida Strait, the first adventurers among what was to be a flood of 124,789 new refugees from Cuba began their crossing to the United States. In the early days of that spring, when the total of refugees was projected at 5,000 and the arriving boats at Key West were greeted with shouts and tears of joy, it seemed that the transition to American life would be so easy. President Carter caught the mood of the Cuban community here when he said in...

Titles1016 KILLED IN SHELLING OF LEBANON PORT CITY BY CHRISTIAN MILITIASP.S.C. SAYS RECORD CON ED RISE WAS VITAL TO ATTRACT INVESTMENTTHE KOCH METHOD FOR WINNING AUDIENCES AND INFLUENCING VOTERSREHNQUIST REJECTS REQUEST TO EXTEND LOS ANGLES BUSINGTERRORISTS SOUGHT IN THE WOUNDING OF TWO OFFICERSPOLISH-AMERICAN EASTER HAS SPECIAL SYMBOLISMFOR NEW YORK, A DAY OF PLAY AND FOR PRAYERNISSAN BRINGS FOREIGN WAYS TO TENNESSEENews Summary; MONDAY, APRIL, 20, 1981Quotation of the Day
16 KILLED IN SHELLING OF LEBANON PORT CITY BY CHRISTIAN MILITIAS
Christian militia forces today shelled the southern Lebanese port of Sidon, killing at least 16 residents and wounding more than 60, according to Lebanese authorities. A few hours after the shelling, Maj. Saad Haddad, commander of the Israeli-supported Christian militias, suffered an apparent heart attack and was rushed to a hospital in Israel, Western diplomats here and in Jerusalem reported. The 42-year-old Major Haddad was said tonight to be in intensive care and to be doing ''satisfactorily.''
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
P.S.C. SAYS RECORD CON ED RISE WAS VITAL TO ATTRACT INVESTMENT
The recent decision by New York State's Public Service Commission to give the Consolidated Edison Company the full 15 1/2 percent rate increase it had requested was shaped in large part by a growing mood among regulators that they must help bolster flagging investor confidence in the public utility industry. ''For the public regulators, it's a whole new ball game,'' said Commissioner Anne F. Mead, who has served on the commission since 1975. ''Given the state of the economy and the competition for capital in today's markets, we have to send some signals to the investment community.'' Consumer advocates and...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
THE KOCH METHOD FOR WINNING AUDIENCES AND INFLUENCING VOTERS
It was, in most regards, a routine performance. A few nights ago Edward I. Koch spoke for an hour before 500 people who filled the lounge of the Yale Club. By the time he finished, he had demonstrated - more graphically perhaps than he had in some time - the many facets of a carefully constructed, combative political style that delights some voters and infuriates others, leaves friends wondering aloud, ''How does he do it?'' and foes asking, ''How does he get away with it?'' On this occasion the Mayor, as one of his aides put it, happened to be...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
REHNQUIST REJECTS REQUEST TO EXTEND LOS ANGLES BUSING
Supreme Court Justice William H. Rehnquist tonight rejected an appeal by the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People to halt the dismantling tomorrow morning of a mandatory busing program for school desegregation here. Justice Rehnquist said in Washington that it would be inappropriate for him to grant an emergency order to block the scrapping of the busing program without hearing the Los Angeles Board of Education present its views on the N.A.A.C.P.'s plea. ''Without granting or denying applicants' request,'' Justice Rehnquist said he was asking the school board to submit an answer to the N.A.A.C.P.'s petition by Wednesday...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
TERRORISTS SOUGHT IN THE WOUNDING OF TWO OFFICERS
Two men who were members of the Black Liberation Army are being sought as ''prime suspects'' in the wounding of two New York City officers, one critically, last Thursday, the Police Department said this morning. The two men, whose names were not released, became the leading suspects when the police found their fingerprints in a van used by the officers' assailants, according to Alice McGillion, deputy police commissioner for public information. The search for the two suspects in the shooting of the officers is nationwide but has been most intense in Newark because of the identification of the two suspects...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
POLISH-AMERICAN EASTER HAS SPECIAL SYMBOLISM
A traditional Polish hymn, ''Wesoly Nam Dzis Dzien Nastal,'' which means ''A very happy day has now dawned on us,'' greeted the newly risen sun at masses in Wallington, N.J., yesterday - a day in which children in new spring clothes helped their mothers arrange dining tables with pisanki and babki, sugar lambs and ham. It was Easter at the Church of the Most Sacred Heart of Jesus, whose parishioners are 80 percent Polish-American, and where the Day of Resurrection is celebrated with all the smells, sights and sounds of a small parish in Poland. But this year, the symbols...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
FOR NEW YORK, A DAY OF PLAY AND FOR PRAYER
Everywhere he went in his coat of many colors on Easter Sunday, people stopped Antonio Perez to ask him questions and take his picture. At 55th Street, as a throng of people and a swirl of smoke from vendors' carts eddied down the long rich canyon of Fifth Avenue, he and Norma Lieber came together in ecumenical admiration. Mr. Perez, a Roman Catholic who emigrated 14 years ago from Cuba, had sewn together a coat and a hat of thousands of buttons, and worn them in from Elizabeth, N.J., to walk in the Easter Parade.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
NISSAN BRINGS FOREIGN WAYS TO TENNESSEE
The boys in Detroit are not going to believe this. Marvin Runyon, an old Detroiter, one of those real auto men who started on the assembly line, was sitting in his office, a converted Air Force barracks, and pondering management techniques: Should the workers at the new plant do calisthenics for 15 minutes before work? Should the new plant give workers their pay only partly in wages and the rest in bonuses if quality and productivity meet expectations? And the company's insistence on no inspections, the notion that good workers perform to expectations, that inspections are unnecessary, will this work...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
News Summary; MONDAY, APRIL, 20, 1981
International Saudia Arabia will not raise oil prices or reduce its production until other petroleum-exporing nations reduce their prices significantly, Sheik Ahmed Zaki Yamani, the Saudi oil minister, said. ''We think it went too far,'' he said. ''It's not in the interest of the international community, and therefore we want it to come down a little bit lower.'' His statements represented Saudia Arabia's most pointed threat so far in its struggle with other members of the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries over oil prices. (Page A1, Column 6.) A port in southern Lebanon was shelled by Christian militia forces, leaving...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Quotation of the Day
''In Poland they will be celebrating Easter as they have not since 1939. It is a new spring in their lives that they weren't able to celebrate before.'' -The Rev. Stanley J. Adamczyk, pastor of the Church of the Most Sacred Heart of Jesus in Wallington, N.J. (B1:1.)

Billy Carter Asserts No Loan Funds Were Used for Brother's Campaign

Iran's First Passover TV Show Helps Ease Jews’ Fears of Future
Iran's Govt‐controlled TV station televises 1st program on Passover and its meaning to Jews; program helps ease fears of Iran's 70,000 Jews, who are worried about Ayatollah Khomeini Govt's strong anti‐Zionist attitude and its vehement support of Arab cause; Jews are concerned about breakdown in law and order, summary trials and executions of political and military figures from Shah's regime and restrictive attitude of Islamic Repub; Jewish community consists mostly of merchants, businessmen and professionals, who trace their Iranian roots to 5th century BC; is split on how to deal with Govt's anti‐Israel campaign; delegation recently met with Prime Min...

Peking's Envoy Says New York Will Get First China Trade Office
Chinese Ambassador Chai Zemin tells Washington, DC, luncheon of New York City business executives that China will establish in city its 1st American office for international trade; officials are guardedly optimistic; China's recent withdrawal from certain trade projects after expressing initial enthusiasm cited; its desire to establish consulates in Houston and San Francisco, presumably to transact trade negotiations with businesses in those areas, noted; Amer request for consulates in Canton and Shanghai for similar purposes noted; New York‐New Jersey Port Authority Chairman Alan Sanger says office would enable city to deal directly with China on trade; luncheon was organized by...

invading force of Tanzanian soldiers and Ugandan exiles capture Kampala, toppling Amin and ending his 8‐yr rule; thousands greet invaders as liberators; Amin denies that Govt has been overthrown, radio broadcast; Tanzanian Pres Nyerere reptdly will have large say in Ugandan affairs and many have assumed that Nyerere would push to reinstate former Pres Milton Obote; most observers now feel Nyerere will not want visibility that would come with hand‐picking country's next Pres; illus; map (L)

SOUTH AFRICA OUSTS 3 U.S. EMBASSY AIDES, CHARGING AIR SPYING
Prime Min P W Botha announces that S Africa has ordered number of US Embassy personnel expelled for allegedly using US Amb William B Edmondson's aircraft to conduct aerial espionage over wide areas of country, including 'sensitive installations,' news conf; says Amb's aircraft was converted for use as spy plane by installation of aerial-survey camera under co-pilot's seat; says Foreign Min Roelof F Botha has informed Edmondson that embassy members must leave S Africa within wk; State Dept identifies embassy members as mil attache Col Alvan M Crews, asst Air Force attache Maj Bernd McConnell and plane's crew chief Sgt...

Zambia Reports Raid By Rhodesian Troops On Nkomo Residence
Radio Zambia repts Rhodesian commando force attacked Nkomo's residence and other bldgs in Lutaka, Zambia (S)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Schlesinger Would Spend 2.5 Billion on Solar Power
Energy Sec Schlesinger recommends that US spend $2.5 billion to stimulate development of solar energy in mid-80's, with aim of having nation get about 12% of its energy from solar power by yr 2000; recommends personal commitment by Pres Carter to solar power, Adm partnership with all sectors of economy to promote it, specific Presidential goals and announcement by Carter in nationwide address or Cong speech (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Tenants to Fight Fuel Surcharges For Apartments
New York State Tenant and Neighborhood Coalition says it will initiate court action to stop landlords from collecting fuel surcharges in rent-stabilized apartments in New York City; New York State Sen Manfred Ohrenstein says he will submit legislation to block surcharges; Assemblyman Edward H Lehner says he will convene hearings to determine whether Board acted properly (M)

2 Killed on South Korean Airliner That Was Forced to Land in Soviet
2 passengers reptdly died during landing on frozen lake near town of Kem, south of Murmansk and 390 mi northeast of Leningrad; Soviet Prime Min Kosygin says 2 others were injured; Tass says airliner ignored instructions to land that were issued by Soviet interceptors; does not indicate whether plane was fired at by interceptors; 1 of dead identified as Japanese; map (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Carter Cuts Total of U.S. Troops To Leave South Korea This Year
Pres Carter announces that he will reduce by 2/3 number of Amer combat troops scheduled to be withdrawn this yr from S Korea; cites Cong inaction on compensatory aid package for S Korea as main reason for action; 1 battalion of about 800 men and 2,600 support personnel will be withdrawn; reaction noted (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
CONFEREES ON ENERGY AGREE ON GAS PRICING, ENDING LONG IMPASSE
Leaders of HR-Sen conf com agree to compromise under which gas price controls will be extended to intrastate mkt and gas prices will rise until it sells for $4.05 a thousand cu ft when controls are lifted in '85; agreement, which still lacks formal com approval, contains provision known as incremental pricing, which is aimed at making indus pay for more expensive supplies; Carter Adm estimates that it will increase producer revenues $21 billion over next 7 yrs when compared with HR formula and $6 billion when compared with Adm proposal; says Sen version would have added $75 billion to...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
President and House Democrats Discuss Tax Cut Without Reform
Pres Carter and 6 liberal Dems from HR Ways and Means Com discuss alternative strategies for achieving passage of tax bill, including possibility of tax cut without tax reform; Repr Richard A Gephardt and others who attended meeting say Carter is optimistic over chances of rescuing some of his revenue-raising tax proposals, but without revisions of tax code; other conf attendees and Cong reprs comment (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U. S. Court Upholds Lengthy Drug Terms
A Federal appeals court ruled yesterday that New York State sentences of up to life in prison for the sale of small amounts of narcotics were at unconstitutionally sevcre.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
ARMS PACT PROGRESS INDICATED IN MOSCOW
US Sec Vance ends 2 days of talks, Moscow, with Soviet Foreign Min Andrei A Gromyko; says relations between both nations have improved vastly since his last visit in Mar '77; discusses brief detention and refusal to grant visa to Irina McClellan; McClellan illus being led from Amer Embassy by Soviet police (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Postal Unit Calls for 15‐Cent Stamp
Postal Rate Comm rejects Carter Adm proposal for 2-tiered rate system that would have kept postage rates on personal lrs at 13c and tentatively votes, 3-1, to raise postage on 1st-doss lrs to 15c; only Simeon Bright, apptd to comm by Pres Carter, voted against 15c rate; recommendation if accepted, could become effective by late May or early June (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Rubens Stolen at Pitti in Florence
Rubens masterpiece The Three Graces and 9 works by 4 less well-known Flemish artists are stolen from Pitti Palace in Florence; museum officials estimate value at more than $1 million; police speculate that thieves had been commissioned to get certain pictures since they left other priceless works behind; illus of Rubens work (M)

Gifts From Parents to ‘Buy’ Places In Professional Schools on the Rise
NY Times survey finds rising demand of Amer youth for limited vacancies in med schools appears to be creating increase in questionable and even illegal efforts to gain admission; finds evidence suggesting parents and friends of prospective students are making millions of dollars in payments to schools to assure acceptance; finds covert bids of as much as $250,000 have been made for 1 place in freshman class of Calif med school; findings detailed; illus (M)

Albany Seeking to Buy or Lease Jails on ikers Island From City
NYS is negotiating to buy or lease entire Rikers Island Prison Complex from city, which would then use money to reopen Tombs in Manhattan and to upgrade and build other jails; details of projected state take-over discussed; aides to Mayor Koch and Gov Carey comment (M)

kIRLINER'S SURVIVORS REACH FINLAND, TELL OF SOVIET JET ATTACK
HELSINKI, Finland, Sunday, April 23&#8212;An American airliner landed here at dawn today, carrying the passengers and most of the crew of the South Korean plane that was forced to land on a frozen lake on Friday by a Soviet jet interceptor. The pilot and navigator of the Korean plane were detained by Soviet authorities at Murmansk.

Vance meets with Communist Party leader Brezhnev; State Dept spokesman says Vance believes he achieved objective of improving overall atmosphere between both nations in preparation for visit by Brezhnev to US later in yr; Vance and Brezhnev illus (M)

Brewers Yield, 4‐3, In 3½‐Hour Battle3 Bombers Injured
Old antagonisms and new injuries surfaced in Yankee Stadium yesterday when Manager Billy Martin &#8220;chewed out&#8221; his world&#8208;champion Yankees and. then watched them go 12 innings to defeat the Milwaukee Brewers, 4&#8208;3.

Catcher Breaks Tie In Eighth Inning With First Hit
CHICAGO, April 22&#8212;The New York Mets, who could not manage a hit against Rick Reuschel through five innings, rallied late in the game today at. Wrigley Field to heat the Cubs, 3&#8208;2, before a chilled crowd of 13,785.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
en a Damaged Heart Is Part of the Vacation Luggage
More and more people with heart conditions are traveling throughout the United States and even to exotic, faraway places. While it has not been unusual for businessmen to travel after heart attacks and with other cardiac ailments, cardiologists and heart surgeons are noting that a small but growing number of patients are traveling simply for pleasure, to vacation away from home.

Some Israeli Politicians Soliciting Funds in U.S. Without Registering
Number of Israeli politicians, including Yigal Yadin, who heads new Dem Movement for Change, Maj Gen Ariel Sharon, who heads Shlomzion Party, and Menahem Begin, head of Likud bloc, and their assocs have been soliciting money in US for their pol campaigns; moves are legal under Israeli and US law, but Justice Dept says Sharon and Yadin have failed to register, as required, as foreign agents; Yadin's fund-raising activities in US have been conducted by Rabbi Herbert Friedman, former exec vp of UJA, who is now living in Israel, and by Israeli businessman Meir Amit; Amer banker Samuel W Sax...

OPEN BORDER STRAINS U.S.‐MEXICO RELATIONS

INDIA WILL MODERATE BIRTH‐CURB PROGRAM
Health and Family Planning Min Raj Narain says India's birth control program will be moderated to eliminate all elements of complusion; says new Govt will halt cash incentive payments that were paid for more than decade to Indians undergoing sterilization operations; expects Govt to increase number of contraceptive devices and to encourage other methods of preventing conception, including abstinence; some public-health officials see signs of backlash to sterilization program as result of compulsion by Gandhi regime (M)

SOVIET INDICATES U.S. MUST TAKE INITIATIVE IN MENDING RELATIONS
USSR, through Pravda and Izvestia, holds it up to Carter Adm to take initiative in repairing damage to Soviet-Amer relations from failure of Moscow arms limitation talks; charge Amer deception at talks and distortion of stands in statements by Carter and Sec Vance later; Tass however repts Carter statement US will do its utmost to strengthen ties; Western diplomats in Moscow speculate on events (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
New York Accused Of Failure to Spend U.S. Community Aid
NYC coalition contends Beame adm has been inept in handling community-development funds and that decayed housing has not been rehabilitated; holds funds have been spent for adm expenses and service programs rather than for badly needed physical improvements in housing (S)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Drug Will Be Used On Humans to Test Cancer Prevention
Natl Cancer Inst lung cancer branch chief Dr Michael B Sporn says inst is planning 1st test on humans of drug that might be able to prevent certain types of cancer, Amer Cancer Soc seminar for science writers; drug, synthetic of chem that is cousin of vitamin A, will be given to 100 persons who face high probability of developing bladder cancer (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Afrikaners Dig In Against Threat to Their Rule
Afrikaners strengthening defenses against mounting threat to white supremacy; Prime Min Vorster has commissioned development of 'total strategy' to stave off pressures for majority rule (L)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
CARTER AIDES TO MAP NEW POLICY ON ALIENS
Carter Adm officials to begin shaping new US immigration policy that could have significant consequences for estimated 6- to 8-million illegal aliens in US, employers and native-born workers; areas covered will include whether amnesty should be granted to most illegal aliens, whether sanctions should be imposed on employers who knowingly hire illegal aliens, whether all workers should be required to carry counterfeit-proof identification cards and what strategies should be developed to stop flow of illegals across unguarded borders (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
City. U. a Remedial‐Writing Leader
Article on remedial-writing program instituted at each of 17 components of CUNY to help students improve writing skills; notes Mina P Shaughnessy, dir of CUNY's Instructional Research Center, along with several faculty members, has been experimenting with several techniques in this area; various remedial-writing techniques discussed; Shaughnessy and several teachers comment; excerpts from English placement exams; Shaughnessy por (L)

Miss Hearst to Cooperate In Prosecution of Harrises
Alameda County (Calif) Asst Dist Atty Alex Selvin, following Emily and William Harris being formally charged with kidnapping of Patricia Hearst, discloses that Miss Hearst's defense atty Albert Johnson told him that the hat agreed to testify against Harrises and other members of Symbionese Liberation Army in apparent move to gain judicial leniency in number of cases in which the it involved; Marin County (Calif) Dist Atty Bruce Bales also discloses that Miss Hearst has admitted to involvement in bombing of 2 sheriff's cars last Aug and, in exchange for immunity, is prepared to testify against others; Hearst reptdly is...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Minot, N.D., Braces for Its Fifth Flood in 7 Years
Minot, ND, braces for its 5th flood in 7 yrs as Souris River begins to crest as result of heavier-than-normal spring runoff from winter snows; city's efforts to cope with expected flood described; map; illus (L)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
KISSINGER WARNS SYRIAN ACTS TEST ISRAEL'S PATIENCE
Apr 15: Kissinger says recent Syrian mil movements into Lebanon are 'getting very close to the borderline' of Israeli tolerance, testimony before Sen subcom; says 'we're walking through a mine field' in efforts to help promote pol solution to Lebanese crisis and to prevent Syrian-Israeli clash that could start new Middle East war; says Israel agrees with US that limited Syrian intervention has not threatened its security; acknowledges that he was overruled by Ford on question of additional $550-million in aid to Israel (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
BEAME TO RESIST LAW THAT ORDERS SCHOOL FUND RISE
Beame submits city's latest austerity budget and announces that he will wage ct fight to resist new legis mandate for spending $150-million more on educ than city reptdly can afford; notes 'substantial thousands' of city workers will have to be dismissed if mandate is upheld; estimates educ will take 18c of every budget dollar; chart showing composition of required budget reductions by agency and other city functions (S)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Moscow Is Said to Modify Expectations of Detente
Article on US-USSR detente cites assessment by Soviet Govt source of US Presidential candidates; notes real favorite among official circles seems to be Sen Hubert Humphrey (S)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Stavisky Bill Backers Act To Ease Burden on City
Supporters of Stavisky-Goodman school-aid bill plan for amendments that will assure more money to schools without necessarily binding NYC to allot a fixed portion of its budget to educ; Sen Goodman reiterates contention of law's supporters that it was principally intended to serve as basis for future bargaining on school-aid formula between city, Educ Bd and and teachers' union; controversy surrounding passage of bill recalled; NYC Mayor Beame calls bill 'unconst and unenforceable' and says city will challenge it in ct; Stavisky por (L)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Big Power Plan Dropped After Long Fight in Utah
Southern Calif Edison, San Diego Gas & Elec and Ariz Public Service cancel $3.5-billion Kaiparowits power project in southern Utah, citing econ, regulatory and environmental problems; Sierra Club exec dir Michael McCloskey, Gov Calvin L Rampton and Southern Calif Edison vp William R Gould comment; maps showing area to be serviced by project and project's proximity to 8 natl parks; illus (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Caretakers Named For Hughes Affairs
Howard Hughes attys, admitting for 1st time they cannot find will, get cts in Tex, Calif and Nov to appt temporary admrs of holdings in those states; Houston judge appts aunt Mrs Annette R Lummis (Mrs Frederick R Loomis) and her son William R; Los Angeles ct appts Richard C Gano, a cousin; Las Vegas ct names 1st Natl Bank of Nev; Houston judge is Neil A Lake, and lawyer, acting for Andrews Kurth Campbell & Jones, is William P Miller; Hughes relatives identified (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
A Good Friday Hymn Being Questioned
portion of RC liturgy for Good Friday that was apparently overlooked in recent efforts to remove bias against Jews from ritual is becoming subject of growing concern among those who believe it contains seeds of anti-Semitism; ancient hymn, called Improperia, or 'reproaches,' consists of works of rebuke against people whose identity is clearly the Jews; Rabbi Marc Tanenbaum, Bishop James S Rausch, Revs Frederick McMannus, John Sheerin, Thomas F Rea, Gerard Sloyan and Msgr John M Oesterreicher comment (M)

Jackson's Strategy Hampered by Lack Of Federal Subsidy
Jackson's campaign is hampered by Sen delay on bill to reconstitute Fed Election Comm, and thus resume dispersal of matching funds; latest repts show little money on hand to conduct planned massive campaign in Pa; Jackson meets with small groups of supporters in effort to raise enough money to make at least strong organizational effort in state (M)

India to Send Ambassador To China, First in 1 5Y ears
L L Mohrotra, Indian charge d'affaires in Peking, says that Han Nien-lung, Deputy of Min of Foreign Affairs, has conveyed to him China's agreement to Indian proposal to re-establish diplomatic relations (S)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Syrian Move Into Lebanon Laid to a Rift With Leftists
Apr 16: Western diplomats say that in direct diplomatic contacts and through Jordanian King Hussein, who was in Washington last wk, Syrians were implored by State Dept to keep down their troops in Lebanon and to stay north of Damascus-Beirut line, meaning well away from Israeli border; both wishes were respected; Syrian Pres Assad reptdly warned Arafat that if his Al Fatah guerrilla orgn did not support Syria in Lebanon, Syria would not only cut off arms to Palestinians but would adopt 'Egyptian policy,' meaning it would cease to support Palestinian cause (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Fast Oil‐Reserve Buildup Is Supported by President
Pres Ford, in move favoring rapid buildup of US strategic oil reserve, seeks Cong authorization to commit $871-million of outlays for oil, storage facilities and studies by Sept '77; Office of Mgt and Budget sought to reduce rate of buildup and shift part of cost of stored crude oil to oil cos; Fed Energy Adm urged rapid buildup, asserting that Budget Office plan was extralegal and gimmick for concealing true cost of storage; Budget Dir James T Lynn and Deputy Energy Admr John A Hill reptdly shouted at each other at Mar 24 Cabinet meeting; Strategic Petroleum Reserve Dir Robert...
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
U.S. SUITS ALLEGE SEX BIAS ON LOANS TO HOME BUYERS
WASHINGTON, April 15&#8212;The Justice Department, in its first such actions under a 1974 law, charged two mortgage lenders today with discrimination against women.
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
LEBANESE RESUME FIGHTING AS SYRIA SEEKS AN ACCORD
fighting flares in Beirut and mts to east; As Saiqa leader Zuheir Mohsen repts his forces and Palestine Liberation Army shell rightist-held towns of Bolonia and Bikfaya in retaliation for Christian seizure of Dhur el-Shuweir in defiance of local cease-fire; rightists charge leftists seek to sabotage Syrian peace initiatives; leftists charge rightists hope to justify further Syrian armed presence; Arafat reptdly blames rightists for increased fighting; leaves for Damascus with high-level Palestinian delegation; Jumblat reptdly gave him free hand in talks; charges Syria blocks food shipments (M)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Honor Code at West Point Focus of Renewed Turmoil
article on scandal discusses case of Cadet Stephen Verr, who charges harassment by officers and cadets who objected to his being cleared of honor-code violation; dispute over Verr, whose alleged 'lie' eventually reached office of Supt Lt Gen Sidney Berry, has focused attention on fairness and need for code, which prohibits lying, cheating or stealing or toleration of those who do; Verr illus (L)

U.S., Stung in Angola, Forges Africa Policy
Ford Adm officials says that possibility of armed conflict between Ethiopia and Somalia appears to be a yr off; conflict centers on French Territory of Afars and Issas, with port city of Djibouti (S)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
Yankees Win First Game in Rebuilt Stadium
Rebuilt Yankee Stadium reopened Apr 15 before a sellout crowd of 54,010 persons who watch NY Yankees defeat Minnesota Twins 11-4; many sports and pol celebrities attend; some comment; illus (L)
NYT article · NYT archive search · Wikipedia search
KIBBEE PUTS OFF PAYING SUPPLIERS
CUNY Chancellor Robert J Kibbee, in defiance of City Comptroller Harrison J Goldin's orders, plans to postpone until May submission of vouchers to city for payment to univ's suppliers because money is needed to pay Apr salaries of teachers and other employes; takes action because of reptd shortage of $2-million needed to meet Apr 30 payroll; development constitutes latest volley in a complicated round of maneuvering by city, state. Higher Educ Bd and Kibbee's adm over future financing of univ; Kibbee por (M)

Sources: Hebcal, Extreme Weather Watch, NOAA daily summaries, New York Times Archive API, New York Times Article Search API, Wikimedia On This Day events, Good Eggs, OfficialData CPI table, BLS inflation calculator note.
Calendar note: this uses the first and second daytime festival dates in the diaspora calendar, not the prior evening seder start.
